items.title: Debugging Kubernetes Services with KFtray’s HTTP Logs and VS Code REST Client Extension
items.author: Henrique Cavarsan
items.content:
Learn how to debug Kubernetes services using KFtray’s HTTP logging with VS Code’s REST Client extension to capture and replay requests.

If you’ve worked with Kubernetes, you already know the debugging headaches it can cause. But what makes it particularly difficult?

First, there’s the distributed architecture. Your applications run across multiple nodes and namespaces, with traffic flowing through various system layers.

Second, environments constantly change. Pods start, stop, and reschedule themselves. Configuration changes happen frequently.

And production environments? They add a whole new level of complexity. Issues often manifest differently in production due to scale, load, or configuration variations. Security policies typically restrict direct access to services.

Have you ever found a bug in development that behaves completely differently in production? You’re not alone.

Most developers rely on a few standard approaches when debugging Kubernetes services. Each has its strengths — and limitations.

Tools like Postman, Insomnia, and curl let you test endpoints directly. They’re useful for simple tests, but they come with constraints:

  • Each request must be manually created
  • They can’t capture live traffic patterns
  • You often need multiple tools during a debugging session

These tools work well in controlled situations but may fall short when dealing with complex, real-world traffic patterns.

Proxy tools like Charles Proxy and mitmproxy provide deeper traffic visibility. But there’s more to it:

  • Certificate management can be complex
  • Workflow integration isn’t always smooth
  • They sometimes capture too much traffic

While useful, these tools often feel disconnected from your development environment, requiring constant context switching.

Low-level tools like Wireshark offer comprehensive traffic inspection but generally provide more information than needed:

  • They have a steep learning curve
  • The level of detail can be overwhelming
  • They lack application context

These tools are great at network-level debugging but can make application-level analysis more complex than necessary.

I’ve found that KFtray offers an approach to Kubernetes debugging by adding HTTP traffic logging capabilities to standard port-forwarding.

It functions as a proxy layer that:

  • Records HTTP traffic through port-forwards
  • Maintains standard port-forward functionality
  • Integrates with existing development workflows

This approach provides additional visibility into service interactions while using familiar port-forwarding mechanics.

You might be asking yourself: “How does this actually work with VS Code?” That’s what i’ll explore next.

When you enable HTTP logging for a port-forward configuration in KFtray, a series of events unfolds:

  1. Start a port forward with http logging option enabled
  2. The app itself captures incoming HTTP requests
  3. Requests are forwarded to your Kubernetes service
  4. Responses are captured on their return journey
  5. Complete request-response pairs are logged with correlation IDs

This process enables traffic capture without requiring application changes or additional debugging tools.

And these logs are formatted in a way that works seamlessly with VS Code.

Setting up HTTP logging in KFtray is an simple process that begins with downloading and installing KFtray v0.16.1 or later from the GitHub releases page. Once you have KFtray installed, you’ll need to configure your port-forward settings.

Start by launching KFtray and creating a new port-forward configuration. You’ll need to specify your Kubernetes environment details, including your desired context and the namespace where your service runs. Make sure to enter the correct service name — this is the Kubernetes service you want to monitor.

Next, configure your port settings. The local port is where you’ll access the service on your machine, while the remote port is where the service runs in your Kubernetes cluster. Give your configuration a descriptive name that helps you identify it easily later. This name will be particularly helpful when you’re managing multiple port-forwards.

With your configuration in place, enable the port-forward by toggling the switch in the KFtray interface. Then, click the menu icon next to your port-forward configuration and enable HTTP logging. This simple step activates the logging feature for your selected port-forward.

KFtray will now automatically capture all HTTP traffic flowing through your port-forward. The logs are saved to $HOME/.kftray/http_logs/, and each log file is named using your configuration ID and local port for easy reference.

Now that you have logging set up, let’s explore what makes these logs particularly valuable for debugging and development.

The HTTP logs generated by KFtray are structured in a specific format that’s compatible with VS Code’s REST Client extension. Each entry contains complete details about the HTTP interaction:

# ----------------------------------------# Trace ID: 651bc5a5-fb13-46ec-b471-473f08a1f828
# Request at: 2025-03-05T16:56:00.076606+00:00
GET /gzip HTTP/1.1Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: deflate, gzip
User-Agent: Test# ----------------------------------------
# Trace ID: 651bc5a5-fb13-46ec-b471-473f08a1f828
# Response at: 2025-03-05T16:56:00.079206+00:00HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *Content-Length: 150
Content-Type: application/json; encoding=utf-8
Date: Wed, 05 Mar 2025 16:56:00 GMT  "headers": {      "*/*"    "Accept-Encoding": [    ],      "127.0.0.1:8787"    "User-Agent": [    ]  "origin": "127.0.0.1:56846",}

The log format includes several key components:

  • : Each request-response pair shares a unique identifier
  • : Useful for identifying performance issues
  • : Includes headers, status codes, and body content

This format is what makes the logs directly usable with VS Code’s REST Client.

The REST Client extension for VS Code is a powerful tool for testing HTTP requests. When combined with KFtray’s logs, it creates a workflow for debugging Kubernetes services.

  • Install the REST Client extension from the VS Code marketplace
  • Open a KFtray-generated log file in VS Code
  • The extension automatically recognizes HTTP requests in the file
  • Use the “Send Request” button to replay any captured request
Note: You can modify parameters, headers, or body content before replaying a request,making it easy to test different scenarios.
Note: This approach works alongside existing debugging tools rather than replacing them.It can complement your current development workflow.

This integration allows you to conduct debugging within your code editor, potentially reducing the need to switch between multiple tools.

Here are some common scenarios in which these tools can be useful.

Production services sometimes experience sporadic errors that are difficult to reproduce. This approach can help:

  • Enable HTTP logging for the service’s port-forward
  • Monitor the application until the error occurs
  • Review the HTTP logs for failing requests:
# ----------------------------------------# Trace ID: 5525d312-5582-46ad-85d1-0fd3710f824e
# Response at: 2025-03-01T15:34:09.901304+00:00
# Took: 144 ms
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
Content-Type: application/json  "error": "Failed to connect to database",
  "code": "DB_CONNECTION_ERROR"
}
  • Use VS Code REST Client to replay the exact request to verify the error pattern

This method can help make intermittent failures more reproducible and easier to debug.

Microservice architectures create complex communication patterns that can be difficult to visualize. HTTP logging helps by capturing traffic across multiple service port-forwards, recording complete request and response cycles, maintaining trace ID correlation across services, and providing timing data for each interaction. This visibility can help identify communication patterns and potential areas for optimization.

Authentication systems typically involve multiple steps of token exchange and validation. With HTTP logging, you can:

  • Record complete authentication request sequences
  • Preserve header information and token data
  • Replay requests with modified parameters
  • Maintain the full context of authentication flows

All that being said, the real value comes from how these tools fit into your existing development workflow.

Combining KFtray’s HTTP logging with VS Code’s REST Client extension offers an additional option for debugging Kubernetes services. This approach provides a way to capture and replay HTTP traffic that can simplify some debugging scenarios.

The format compatibility between KFtray’s logs and VS Code REST Client creates a workflow that includes:

  • Capturing service interactions with context
  • Documenting intermittent issues
  • Providing request replay capabilities
  • Supplementing existing debugging approaches

i believe this combination of tools could be helpful in certain Kubernetes debugging scenarios. i’m curious to see how you might apply this approach to your own debugging challenges.

Feedback and contributions are welcome.

items.title: F5 Text-to-Speech (F5-TTS): A Compact and Efficient Voice Cloning Model to Run on Your Local…
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
F5 Text-to-Speech (F5-TTS): A Compact and Efficient Voice Cloning Model to Run on Your Local Machine

F5 Text-to-Speech (F5-TTS) is a powerful open-source tool that converts written text into high-quality, lifelike audio. What makes it particularly impressive is its , which requires only a small sample of audio to accurately replicate a voice — a feature that, as of today, is remarkably advanced. This makes it an excellent choice for users looking to clone voices with minimal data, which is ideal for applications such as personalized voiceovers, virtual assistants, and accessibility tools.

However, if your goal is purely text-to-speech (TTS) generation without the need for cloning, other tools might be more suitable. There are commercially established alternatives, such as , which offer a high-quality and widely used voice synthesis service, though with an associated cost. On the other hand, there are , like , which provide satisfactory quality without relying on online services, allowing greater control over the processing.

If you’re interested in exploring different options, I have tested various TTS models and shared my insights in other posts — check them out at my Medium page.

  • High-quality audio synthesis using pre-trained models.
  • for speed, pitch, and denoising.
  • for easy usage through Gradio.
  • Compatibility with GPU-accelerated environments for efficient processing.

To set up the F5-TTS tool, you can refer to the following resources for its code, pre-trained models, and documentation:

  • : Ubuntu 24.04 via WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux)
  • : Python 3.12.4
  • : ffmpeg for handling audio files.

Setting Up F5-TTS: Step-by-Step

Here, I present instructions for setting up and running F5-TTS on your local machine.

Step 1: Create a Virtual Environment

Using a virtual environment ensures that dependencies are isolated and managed effectively. Follow these steps:

mkdir -p <your-project-path>cd <your-project-path>source venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip

Choose the installation method based on your GPU type:

pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124
pip install torch==2.5.1+rocm6.2 torchaudio==2.5.1+rocm6.2 --extra-index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm6.2

Step 3: Install PortAudio

Update your system and install the required packages:

sudo apt update -ysudo apt install ffmpeg portaudio19-dev  -y

Install F5-TTS directly from its GitHub repository:

pip install git+https://github.com/SWivid/F5-TTS.gitpip list  # Verify installed dependencies

Run the F5-TTS Gradio web interface:

f5-tts_infer-gradio --port 7860 --host 0.0.0.0

Using F5-TTS: Basic Voice Cloning and Synthesizing Text to High-Quality Audio

In this section, we’ll explore the simplest way to use F5-TTS for voice cloning and text-to-speech synthesis.

On the main interface of the application, ensure you are in the Basic TTS tab. Next, upload a reference audio file by clicking the appropriate upload button. This audio will serve as the basis for voice cloning.

Step 2: Define the Text to Synthesize

In the Text to Generate section, input the text you want to synthesize.For example:

The gentle symphony of nature unfolds in every corner of our world.

To generate the audio, click . After processing, listen to the output and make adjustments if necessary.

If you’re satisfied with the result, use the button to save the audio file.

Optional: Advanced Configuration

If you want to refine the audio further, you can experiment with the following advanced settings:

  • : Adjust the playback speed of the synthesized voice.
  • : Fine-tune noise filtering for clearer audio.
  • : Remove pauses or silences, especially useful for longer text inputs.

Using F5-TTS: Advanced Voice Cloning and Synthesizing Text to High-Quality Audio

In this section, we will explore the advanced method of voice cloning. This approach allows you to assign specific emotional tones to segments of speech, enabling the generation of varied and expressive audio outputs.

The system can synthesize speech using the designated emotional tone. If no tone is specified, the model defaults to the speech type. Each specified tone will remain in use until another is indicated.

Step 1: Prepare Reference Audio

Before synthesizing speech with emotional tones, you’ll need to record multiple audio samples with different vocal intonations to use as references. For instance, create recordings for each tone with the suggested content below. Save each sentence as a separate file:

{Regular} Hello, I'd like to order a sandwich please.{Surprised} What do you mean you're out of bread?
{Sad} I really wanted a sandwich though...

Step 2: Upload Each Audio File

In the application interface, navigate to the Multiple Speech tab.

For each reference audio:

  • Add the emotional tone name in the field (e.g., Regular, Surprised, Sad).
  • Upload the corresponding file in the  field.
  • Enter the spoken content from the reference audio in the  field.
  • Click to save this tone configuration.

Repeat this process for all your audio samples.

Step 3: Define Text to Generate

In the Text to Generate section, input the text you wish to synthesize. Unlike simple text-to-speech generation, here you can include emotional tone tags to indicate the desired tone for each segment. If no tag is provided, the system will use the tone by default.

{Regular} Voice cloning technology has advanced significantly in recent years, allowing us to replicate human speech with astonishing accuracy.{Surprised} Wait, are you saying this isn't a real person speaking right now?
{Sad} It's incredible... but also a little unsettling to think that voices can be copied so perfectly.

To generate the audio, click . After processing, listen to the output and refine the text or tones as needed.

Once satisfied, use the button to save the final audio file.

F5-TTS is a remarkable tool that makes high-quality text-to-speech synthesis, including voice cloning, accessible to everyone. By following the steps outlined in this guide, you can set up the tool and experiment with its capabilities, from simple text generation to advanced emotional voice cloning.

Its flexibility and robust performance make it suitable for a wide range of applications, including entertainment, education, accessibility, and more.

Take the leap into the world of advanced TTS synthesis and explore the creative possibilities with F5-TTS!

If you found this guide helpful, stay connected for more insights on AI, cloud security, and AWS automation:

Happy voice cloning with  🚀

items.title: Database isolation levels and locking
items.author: Vaishali Behere
items.content:
Understanding database locking and isolation levels

In this article, the main focus is exploring the depths of the ‘whys’ and ‘hows’ of the ‘I’ (Isolation) in ACID properties of relational databases. The Isolation property ensures that two concurrent transactions do not cause data inconsistencies by interfering with each other. There are different isolation levels provided by the database as part of this property. The higher the isolation level, the better the protection against inconsistencies, but at the cost of overall performance.

Let’s first understand the issues with concurrent transactions in a world where there are no locks and no isolation levels to protect them.

Frequently discussed issues:

    There are two additional critical issues that are rarely discussed:

    • Dirty writes (Never happens! As even the lowest isolation level provides protection from it. We will discuss in detail later in the doc.)
    : The end result of these operations should have been what Transaction 2 intended to do, i.e., read the current price of item A and increment it by 50 because Transaction 1 never made it to the DB. The expected value was 150. Since Transaction 2 read the uncommitted value of Transaction 1 (which later rolled back), the data inconsistency caused the price to increase by 100 instead of the intended increase of 50. Replace ‘price’ with financial accounts, and a dirty read becomes a sin!
    : Transaction 1 read the quantity of item A. In the meantime, another transaction intervened, set the quantity to a different value, and committed. Transaction 1 would not be able to read the same value it read at Step 1 again. Is this really a big deal? Why is this being called an issue? Isn’t this the right behavior? Obviously, you should read the committed value the second time you access it. However, this leads to financial disputes and customer dissatisfaction when a transaction based on the original value is altered in the middle. Therefore, it needs to be addressed effectively wherever applicable.

    Phantom reads (not covering in depth)

    : The phantom reads problem occurs primarily due to new row insertions and not due to updates on the same row. For instance, if a concurrent transaction inserts new rows and a re-read range query is issued, it returns a different number of rows
    : Consider a scenario where an item’s stock quantity was 100. Customer 1 and Customer 2 came in together, and Customer 1 cancelled an order for 10 items, resulting in the quantity being returned to stock. At the same time, another customer placed an order for 100 items, resulting in the quantity being deducted from stock. Ultimately, item A should have 10 items left in the warehouse. But due to Transaction 1’s updates getting lost, 10 items can never be retrieved, despite the item being available.

    Dirty writes(not covering in depth because even the lowest of isolation level prevents it)

    Issue: Expected value should have been what transaction1 intended to do i.e., A=150 as transaction2 never got committed, but dirty writes would cause A’s value to be 200.

    Different type of isolation levels in relational databases

      Which issues can/cannot happen in which isolation levels - NoCannot happen- Yes — Can happen

      Lock and lock rules (MySQL with default InnoDB engine is taken as a reference) :

      Let’s first understand some terminologies related to locking

      Intention locks are table level non blocking locks indicating intention of a transaction to inform concurrent transactions that there is a future intent to acquire granular row level locks so that data consistency can be maintained. (IS) = Intent shared locks, (IX) = Intent exclusive locks.

      Shared Locks (Read locks)Shared locks are the locks acquired for reading the row. It is represented by (S)

      Exclusive Locks (Write locks) Exclusive locks are the locks acquired for modifying the row. One such example is update command. It is represented by (X)

      Locking reads are the reads taking IS or IX on select command. Mentioned in detail in Intention lock section.

      Consistent non locking reads : Consistent non locking reads happen when a read operation is performed using snapshot info. Snapshot is a particular point in time state of the actual table.

      Let’s now understand the constant rules across any isolation levels that bind different locks together :

      Let’s now understand the variable rules across isolation levels that help them solve different concurrent transaction problems :

      • of a row doesn’t involve reading from snapshot i.e. consistent read. areheld until end of transaction.
      • even within the same transaction resets and reads fresh snapshot i.e. if any concurrent transaction commits the updates, consecutive consistent read in the original transaction will have updated data. One important thing to keep in mind is that the default mode for plain SELECT queries in this isolation level is non-locking reads, i.e., no lock is acquired for reads.areheld until end of transaction.
      • Every consistent read in a transaction reads from a snapshot of the committed changes taken when the first consistent read command was issued. i.e., if any concurrent transaction commits updates, consecutive consistent reads in the original transaction will always read the data it initially read. The only exception is reading the updated value in the next read if updates are performed within the same transaction. One important thing to keep in mind is that the default mode for plain SELECT queries in this isolation level is non-locking reads, i.e., no lock is acquired for reads.areheld until end of transaction.
      • If autocommit is off, it converts non-locking reads, i.e., plain SELECTs, to locking reads automatically, i.e., SELECT ... FOR SHARE. If autocommit is on, every plain SELECT is a transaction on its own and hence treated as a non-locking read.areheld until end of transaction.

      Let’s use constant rules and variable rules together for each isolation level to understand how different concurrent transaction issues are tackled

        Read uncommitted / Read committed / Repeatable read / Serializable : Dirty writes is not possible in any isolation level because at step2, once X lock is acquired, it will be held until end of T1. Hence, T2 can never set A to the new value and will have to wait as T1 holds X so no other transaction can hold X till T1 commits/rollbacks.
        1. T2 continues it’s decisions basis A=150
         : Since there is no reading from snapshot, T2 (at Step3) can read the intermediate value set by T1 (at Step2) hence dirty reads.
        Read committed / Repeatable read : Since T1 takes X lock at Step2, and since the refreshed/first read snapshot of T2 has only committed data, T2 at Step3 would still read A=100 hence no dirty reads. The problem of lost updates might arise (will be discussed later in the section) if step4 is commit instead of rollback, but definitely not dirty reads.
         : Since T1 will take X lock at step2 and will hold it until EOT, T2 will be blocked at Step3 due to locking read not compatible with write lock and will have to wait for read until T1 releases the lock at the end of transaction. Hence, dirty reads are prevented.

        Non repeatable reads recap

           : Since there is no reading from snapshot, T1 at step1 and step4 will have different values read. Hence, non repeatable read issue arises.
           : Since snapshot is refreshed for every read, T1 at step1 and step4 will have different values read. Hence, non repeatable read issue arises.
           : Since during first read of T1 at Step1, snapshot is finalised, another read at Step4 will have the same value read at Step1. Hence, it solves non repeatable read issue.
           : Since T1 at step1 will be converted to locking read despite it being the plain select, T2 at step2 will anyway have to wait for T1 to finish hence no chance of non repeatable read issue.
          Skipping this as it deals with inserting new rows altogether and not working on concurrent updates to the same row. This can only be prevented with Serializable isolation level due to gap locks (not in scope as part of this article).
          1. T1 reads A=100 , T2 reads A=100 at the same time
          2. T1 adds quantity to A by 10 i.e. A=110
          3. T2 deducts quantity from A by 100 i.e. A=0
          Read uncommitted / Read committed / Repeatable read : Since step1 has non locking reads, step2,3,4,5 can execute without any hinderance causing lost updates issue.
           : Since T1, T2 at step1 will be locking reads, T1 needing X at step2 will be blocked on T2 to release IS lock acquired at step1 and T2 needing X at step4 will be blocked on T1 to release IS lock acquired at step1 and IS lock release is not going to happen in any transaction until EOT . It is a deadlock! InnoDB behavior would be that T2 will end by getting a at Step4 and T1 will go through. Hence, lost updates issue is prevented.

          Frequently used SQL commands to keep handy for testing different use cases

          set session transaction isolation level read uncommitted; --To set session isolation levelselect @@transaction_isolation --To check the set session isolation level
          select @@global.transaction_isolation --To check global isolation level
          show engine innodb status; --To check if any transaction is waiting on a lock
          start transaction; --To start a transaction
          select * from x where y=z for share; --To get IS lock
          select * from x where y=z for update; --To get IX lock
          rollback; --To rollback the transaction
          commit; --To commit the transaction

          Note — All the examples mentioned in the article have been practically tried and tested.

          More on deadlocks and solving the lost updates problem without using the serializable isolation level in the next article.

items.title: How to Run MySQL in a Docker Container
items.author: Dbschema Pro
items.content:
In this article, we will see a step-by-step tutorial on running a MySQL database in a Docker Container.

1. Downloading a MySQL Server Docker Image

To download the image, open the command line and type this command:

docker pull mysql/mysql-server:latest

The: latest tag will download the latest version of MySQL. If you want do download a specific version, simply replace the (Ex: mysql-server: 8.0)

2. Start a MySQL Container in Docker

The next step is to run a container in Docker with the MySQL image. To do this, execute the next command:

docker run --name=mysql1 -p 3306:3306 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123456 -d mysql/mysql-server:8.0

Let’s break down this command to understand it better:

  • run — will run a new command in a new Docker container
  • –name — will give a name to the new container created
  • -p — will make the internal docker port visible outside docker***-e** — will change the root password. Here you can insert whatever password you want
  • mysql/mysql-server:8.0 — will specify what image to run in the newly created container

To verify if the container was created and running, we can execute a docker ps (process status) command:

CONTAINER ID        IMAGE                      COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS                            PORTS                  NAMES79742fe73ea9        mysql/mysql-server:8.0     "/entrypoint.sh mysq…"   24 hours ago    Up 6 seconds (health: starting)   3306/tcp, 33060/tcp    mysql1

This will list all the running containers as shown above.

You can see the (health: starting) mention in the status column of the above result. After the container is initializedand ready to run, you will see it change to (healthy).

3. Connecting to MySQL Server from within the container

docker exec -it mysql1 mysql -uroot -p

items.title: Create New User in MySQL & Grant Access
items.author: Dbschema Pro
items.content:
Creating a new user in MySQL and granting privileges is very easy. First, you have to open the MySQL command line.

You can do this by reaching the bin directory from Program Files.

cd\Program Files\MySQL\MySQL Server 8.0\bin

This is the usual path for the MySQL bin folder. After you reach the bin folder, execute the next command:

This will require the password set at the installation.

In MySQL, users are a combination of __username`` and __hostname identified by a __`password. The __hostname`` can be replaced with __%`` to allow connections from any host.

3. Create a user for ‘localhost’

Now, from inside the MySQL shell, let’s create a new user for __`localhost`` by executing the next command:

CREATE USER 'user'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';

To grant access to the user, execute:

GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'user'@'localhost';

Finally, we will reload the grant table by executing:

Now let’s break down the commands to understand them better:

  • __CREATE USER ‘user’@’localhost’ - pretty straight forward, this command will tell MySQL to create a new user. Replace __user withthe name you want to attribute. __@’localhost’ specifies that the user can connect only from __localhost``;
  • __IDENTIFIED BY ‘password’` — here we specify the password attributed to that user;
  • __GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES - this will give the user rights to modify the tables, columns, etc. The __ALL PRIVILEGES part specifiesthat the user will have standard privileges;
  • __ON - this part specifies the databases on which the user will have access. The first * means that the user will have access to **all databases**. If you want to grant access on a specific database, replace it with the name. For example,ON dbtest.*`;
  • __TO ‘user’@’localhost’` — identifies the user;
  • __FLUSH PRIVILEGES` — this will tell the server to refresh the grant tables.

4. Create a user that can connect from any host

To give a user the right to connect from any host, we have to replace the __localhostmentions from the previous commands with%`. They will look like this:

CREATE USER 'user'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'user'@'%';

5. Delete a user from the database

To delete a user from the database, we will execute the next command:

DROP USER 'user'@'localhost'

Now you are ready to manage your users in MySQL.

items.title: Automating Multi-Node Docker Swarm Deployment With Terraform & Ansible
items.author: Noel
items.content:
Managing containerized applications at scale requires a robust and automated provisioning mechanism. While Docker Swarm simplifies container orchestration, setting up and configuring Swarm clusters across multiple nodes manually can be -

  • : Inconsistent configurations lead to deployment failures.
  • : Manually provisioning instances, setting up networking, and initializing Swarm takes significant effort.
  • : Scaling and upgrading the cluster without automation introduces operational risks.

To address these challenges, Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform and Ansible provide a powerful combination -

  • handles infrastructure provisioning (e.g., creating VMs, configuring networking).
  • automates post-provisioning configuration (e.g., installing Docker, initializing Swarm, setting up HashiCorp Vault, monitoring, and logging).
  • provides container orchestration, enabling self-healing, service discovery, and load balancing across multiple worker nodes.

This article provides a step-by-step guide to deploying a highly available, multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform and Ansible. The key components include -

✅ Terraform for Infrastructure Provisioning

  • Creating cloud instances (e.g., OpenStack, AWS, Azure, GCP).
  • Defining infrastructure components like security groups, networking, and storage.

✅ Ansible for Configuration Management

  • Installing Docker & setting up Swarm managers and worker nodes.
  • Configuring service discovery, networking, and monitoring.

✅ Docker Swarm for Container Orchestration

  • Running applications in a multi-node cluster.
  • Deploying and managing services across worker nodes.

✅ High Availability (HA) Considerations

  • Implementing multi-manager node setups for fault tolerance.
  • Using HashiCorp Vault for secrets management.
  • Integrating log aggregation and monitoring for real-time insights.

By the end of this article, you’ll learn to implement a fully automated multi-node Docker Swarm cluster running on OpenStack (or any cloud provider), ready for production workloads by following the exact same methods and best practices our DevOps teams at Xinthe do.

Understanding The Tools — Terraform, Ansible & Docker Swarm

Deploying a highly available, automated Docker Swarm cluster requires the integration of three key technologies — Terraform, Ansible, and Docker Swarm. Each of these tools serves a distinct purpose in ensuring scalability, reliability, and automation of the infrastructure and orchestration layer.

  • 1️⃣ Terraform — Infrastructure-As-Code (IaC)

Terraform is a declarative infrastructure provisioning tool that enables developers and DevOps teams to define cloud resources in a repeatable, scalable, and version-controlled manner.

: Works across major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP, OpenStack).

: Tracks changes to infrastructure, preventing manual drifts.

: Reusable modules streamline provisioning at scale.

✅ Terraform In Docker Swarm Deployment

Provision Compute Instances: Launches Swarm manager and worker nodes.

: Defines security groups, firewall rules, and virtual networks.

: Allocates volumes for persistent data storage.

: Reduces manual intervention in infrastructure setup.

Example: Terraform Configuration for launching Swarm nodes on OpenStack -

resource "openstack_compute_instance_v2" "swarm_node" {  name        = "swarm-manager"
  image_name  = "Ubuntu-22.04"
  flavor_name = "m1.medium"
  key_pair    = "swarm-key"
  security_groups = ["default"]    name = "swarm-network"}

In this case, Terraform provisions the infrastructure while Ansible will configure it.

  • 2️⃣ Ansible — Configuration Management & Automation

Ansible is a declarative, agentless automation tool designed to ensure consistent and idempotent configuration across multiple nodes.

: Uses SSH to push configurations.

: Ensures the same configuration is applied every time without unintended changes.

: Supports roles and reusable playbooks.

✅ Ansible In Docker Swarm Deployment

Installs Docker Engine on all nodes.

Initializes the Swarm Cluster and assigns nodes as managers/workers.

Deploys Monitoring & Logging Tools (e.g., Prometheus, ELK Stack).

Example: Ansible Playbook for installing Docker -

- name: Install Docker  hosts: all  tasks:
    - name: Install dependencies
      apt:
        name: ['apt-transport-https', 'ca-certificates', 'curl', 'software-properties-common']
        state: present
    - name: Add Docker repository
      shell: curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | apt-key add -      apt:        state: present

Once Terraform provisions the nodes, Ansible automates software installation and cluster setup.

  • 3️⃣ Docker Swarm — Native Container Orchestration

Docker Swarm is a lightweight alternative to Kubernetes for managing containerized applications. It provides service discovery, fault tolerance, and auto-scaling with less complexity compared to Kubernetes.

Built-in with Docker (No extra installation required).

Lightweight & Simple: Ideal for small-to-medium-scale workloads.

Supports Rolling Updates & Load Balancing automatically.

Distributed & Fault-Tolerant: Multiple managers ensure high availability.

✅ Docker Swarm In Our Deployment

: Control the cluster and schedule services.

: Execute containers and respond to scaling needs.

: Distributes traffic across replicas.

: Ensures seamless application upgrades.

Example: Swarm Initialization using Ansible -

- name: Initialize Docker Swarm  hosts: manager    - name: Create Swarm
      command: docker swarm init --advertise-addr {{ ansible_default_ipv4.address }}

After initialization, worker nodes will join the cluster dynamically, ensuring scalability and high availability.

Combining these tools helps us achieve a fully automated, scalable, and production-ready Swarm cluster with minimal manual intervention.

Designing A Scalable Multi-Node Docker Swarm Architecture

A highly available (HA) Docker Swarm cluster requires careful planning of infrastructure components, security, and networking. This section outlines the key components, best practices for security, and networking considerations to ensure a resilient and scalable deployment.

  • 1️⃣ Infrastructure Components

A multi-node Swarm cluster consists of manager and worker nodes, along with optional load balancers and monitoring nodes for increased performance, reliability, and observability.

Cluster Topology Overview

Key Considerations For Each Component

Control Swarm membership, service scheduling, and high availability.

Use odd-numbered clusters (3, 5, or 7 managers) to prevent split-brain scenarios.

Deploy managers in different availability zones (if on the cloud) for fault tolerance.

Execute containers and scale dynamically based on workload.

Use autoscaling (AWS ASG, Azure VM Scale Sets, GCP Managed Instance Groups) to handle traffic bursts.

Workers can be safely rebooted without impacting cluster integrity.

c) Load Balancer (Optional, But Recommended)

NGINX, HAProxy, or AWS ALB can act as the external traffic entry point.

Routes requests to Swarm services via Docker’s built-in ingress network.

Ensures high availability and failover handling.

d) Monitoring Node (Optional, But Recommended)

Prometheus collects metrics on CPU, memory, and service health.

Grafana visualizes the Swarm cluster’s performance in real-time.

Log aggregation with ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) enables debugging.

  • 2️⃣ Security & Networking Considerations

A secure Docker Swarm deployment ensures data integrity, encrypted communications, and controlled access to services.

🔐 Security Best Practices

🌐 Networking Best Practices

By default, Docker Swarm operates with an internal overlay network to connect services across nodes securely. However, optimizing firewall rules and port access is essential for performance and security.

Restrict 2377 to only manager nodes to prevent unauthorized access.

Ensure worker nodes only expose 7946 and 4789 for internal communication.

Use Cloud Provider Firewalls / AWS Security Groups to block all public access except via the load balancer.

Terraform Example — Secure Network Configuration

The following Terraform configuration provisions a secure VPC and security group for Swarm nodes -

resource "aws_vpc" "swarm_vpc" {  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
resource "aws_subnet" "swarm_private_subnet" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = false
resource "aws_security_group" "swarm_sg" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id    from_port   = 2377    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]  # Allow only internal communication
  }    from_port   = 7946    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  }    from_port   = 4789    protocol    = "udp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  }    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]  # Allow outgoing connections
  }

Provisioning Infrastructure With Terraform

In this section, we will provision a highly available multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform. The infrastructure will be deployed in AWS, but the approach can be adapted for GCP, Azure, or on-premise environments.

  • 1️⃣ Setting Up The Terraform Project Structure

A well-structured Terraform project ensures maintainability and modularity. Below is a recommended directory structure for deploying Docker Swarm with Terraform and Ansible.

terraform-docker-swarm/│── main.tf              # Defines AWS/GCP infrastructure
│── variables.tf         # Input variables for customization
│── outputs.tf           # Outputs like IP addresses
│── ansible/             # Ansible playbooks for configuration
│── inventory.ini        # Hosts inventory for Ansible
│── deploy.sh            # Deployment script

→ Provisions AWS infrastructure (instances, networking, security groups).

→ Stores configurable inputs (e.g., region, instance type, AMI ID).

→ Captures instance IPs for further automation with Ansible.

→ Contains playbooks for setting up Docker Swarm.

→ Automates Terraform deployment and Ansible execution.

  • 2️⃣ Terraform Code For Provisioning Multi-Node Infrastructure

Terraform will be used to provision EC2 instances, configure security groups, and output the necessary information for Ansible.

🔹 main.tf (Example for AWS EC2 Instances)

provider "aws" {  region = "us-east-1"
resource "aws_vpc" "swarm_vpc" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
resource "aws_subnet" "swarm_subnet" {
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id
  cidr_block              = "10.0.1.0/24"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
resource "aws_security_group" "swarm_sg" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id
  # Allow SSH for Ansible automation
  ingress {    to_port     = 22    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] 
  # Allow Docker Swarm traffic
  ingress {    to_port     = 2377    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]    from_port   = 7946    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  }    from_port   = 4789    protocol    = "udp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  }    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
resource "aws_instance" "swarm_nodes" {
  count = 5
  ami   = "ami-12345678" # Ubuntu 22.04 LTS AMI ID
  instance_type = "t3.medium"
  key_name      = "swarm-key"
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.swarm_subnet.id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.swarm_sg.id]
    Name = "Swarm-Node-${count.index + 1}"
  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = ["sudo apt-get update -y"]      type        = "ssh"      private_key = file("~/.ssh/swarm-key.pem")
      host        = self.public_ip
    }}
  • 3️⃣ Variables For Terraform Configuration

variables.tf helps parameterize the infrastructure, making it easier to scale or modify.

variable "aws_region" {  default = "us-east-1"
variable "instance_type" {
  default = "t3.medium"  default = "ami-12345678"  # Replace with latest Ubuntu 22.04 AMI  default = "swarm-key"
  • 4️⃣ Capturing Node IPs For Ansible

Once Terraform provisions the EC2 instances, it outputs the IP addresses for use in the Ansible inventory file.

output "manager_ips" {  description = "Public IPs of Swarm manager nodes"
  value       = aws_instance.swarm_nodes[*].public_ip
}  description = "Public IPs of Swarm worker nodes"
  value       = aws_instance.swarm_nodes[*].public_ip
}
  • 5️⃣ Automating Deployment With deploy.sh

A bash script can orchestrate Terraform and Ansible to ensure a one-command setup.

#!/bin/bash
echo "🚀 Deploying Terraform Infrastructure..."
terraform init
terraform apply -auto-approve
echo "⏳ Waiting for instances to be ready..."
sleep 60  # Allow time for EC2 instances to boot up
echo "📌 Fetching instance IPs..."
terraform output manager_ips > manager_ips.txt
terraform output worker_ips > worker_ips.txt
echo "📝 Generating Ansible inventory..."
echo "[managers]" > ansible/inventory.ini
cat manager_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.ini
echo "[workers]" >> ansible/inventory.ini
cat worker_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.ini
echo "✅ Running Ansible Playbooks..."
cd ansible
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini setup_swarm.yml

Automating Swarm Cluster Configuration With Ansible

In this section, we will use Ansible to automate the configuration of a multi-node Docker Swarm cluster.

The Ansible playbooks will handle -

Installing Docker on all nodes.

Initializing the Swarm cluster on the manager nodes.

Retrieving Swarm join tokens dynamically.

Joining worker nodes to the cluster.

  • 1️⃣ Ansible Inventory File (inventory.ini)

This inventory file defines the Swarm manager and worker nodes. Terraform outputs the public or private IPs, which are dynamically populated into this file.

[managers]192.168.1.10192.168.1.12192.168.1.20

contains the manager nodes responsible for scheduling and orchestrating services.

consists of worker nodes that run the containers but do not manage the cluster.

  • 2️⃣ Installing Docker On All Nodes (install_docker.yml)

This Ansible playbook installs Docker and its dependencies on all nodes.

- name: Install Docker on Swarm Nodes  hosts: managers, workers  tasks:
    - name: Update apt repositories
      apt:
    - name: Install Docker & dependencies
      apt:          - docker.io        state: present
    - name: Enable Docker service
      systemd:        enabled: yes
    - name: Add user to Docker group
      user:        groups: docker

Updates the system repositories to ensure the latest packages.

Installs Docker & Docker Compose on all nodes.

Enables and starts the Docker service so it runs automatically.

Adds the default user (ubuntu) to the Docker group to run Docker commands without sudo.

  • 3️⃣ Initializing Docker Swarm (init_swarm.yml)

The first Swarm manager node must initialize the cluster.

Once initialized, tokens for adding managers and workers are dynamically retrieved.

- name: Initialize Docker Swarm  hosts: managers  tasks:
    - name: Initialize swarm on first manager
      command: "docker swarm init --advertise-addr {{ ansible_host }}"
    - name: Retrieve manager join token
      command: "docker swarm join-token manager -q"      run_once: true
    - name: Retrieve worker join token
      command: "docker swarm join-token worker -q"      run_once: true
    - name: Set manager join token as a fact
      set_fact:
        manager_join_command: "docker swarm join --token {{ manager_token.stdout }} {{ ansible_host }}:2377"
    - name: Set worker join token as a fact
      set_fact:
        worker_join_command: "docker swarm join --token {{ worker_token.stdout }} {{ ansible_host }}:2377"

Initializes Docker Swarm on the first manager (run_once: true ensures only one manager initializes the cluster).

Retrieves the join tokens for additional managers and workers.

Stores the tokens as Ansible facts to be used in the next playbook.

  • 4️⃣ Adding Worker Nodes (join_swarm.yml)

Once the Swarm cluster is initialized, worker nodes must join the cluster.

- name: Join workers to the Swarm cluster  hosts: workers  tasks:
    - name: Join swarm as worker
      command: "{{ hostvars[groups['managers'][0]]['worker_join_command'] }}"

Retrieves the worker join command from the first manager node.

Runs the join command on all worker nodes, registering them into the cluster.

  • 5️⃣ Adding Additional Manager Nodes (join_managers.yml)

If additional manager nodes are needed, they must join using the manager token.

- name: Join additional managers to the Swarm cluster  hosts: managers[1:]  tasks:
    - name: Join swarm as manager
      command: "{{ hostvars[groups['managers'][0]]['manager_join_command'] }}"

Selects all managers except the first (managers[1:]).

Runs the join command to add the new managers to the Swarm cluster.

  • 6️⃣ Automating The Entire Process With deploy.sh

To fully automate the Swarm setup, we create a deployment script that runs Terraform and Ansible in sequence.

#!/bin/bash
echo "🚀 Deploying Terraform Infrastructure..."
terraform init
terraform apply -auto-approve
echo "⏳ Waiting for instances to be ready..."
sleep 60  # Allow EC2 instances to boot up
echo "📌 Fetching instance IPs..."
terraform output manager_ips > manager_ips.txt
terraform output worker_ips > worker_ips.txt
echo "📝 Generating Ansible inventory..."
echo "[managers]" > ansible/inventory.ini
cat manager_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.ini
echo "[workers]" >> ansible/inventory.ini
cat worker_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.ini
echo "✅ Running Ansible Playbooks..."
cd ansible
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini install_docker.yml
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini init_swarm.yml
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini join_swarm.yml
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini join_managers.yml

Deploys Terraform infrastructure.

Extracts the instance IPs from Terraform outputs.

Generates the Ansible inventory file dynamically.

Runs all Ansible playbooks in order to set up Docker Swarm.

  • Final Steps & Next Actions

Once the deployment completes -

✅ Docker Swarm cluster is fully configured.

✅ Manager and worker nodes are added dynamically.

✅ Automation reduces manual intervention and setup errors.

Deploying & Managing Services In Docker Swarm

Once the Docker Swarm cluster is set up, the next step is deploying and managing containerized services efficiently.

✅ Deploying a sample web application.

✅ Scaling services dynamically.

✅ Updating services with zero downtime.

  • 1️⃣ Deploying A Sample Web Application In Swarm

To demonstrate service deployment, we will deploy an Nginx web server across the Swarm cluster.

🔹 Ansible Playbook For Deploying Nginx (deploy_nginx.yml)

This Ansible playbook deploys an Nginx service with 3 replicas, ensuring high availability.

- name: Deploy Web Application in Swarm  hosts: managers  tasks:
    - name: Deploy Nginx service
      command: >        --name nginx        --publish 80:80

Deploys an Nginx container using docker service create.

Ensures high availability with — replicas 3, meaning three instances of Nginx will run across Swarm nodes.

Publishes port 80, making the service accessible via the manager node.

  • 2️⃣ Scaling Services Dynamically

As demand increases, you may need to scale the service dynamically.

This can be done using docker service scale.

Scaling Nginx To 10 Replicas

docker service scale nginx=10

Swarm will automatically distribute new replicas across available worker nodes.

Traffic will be load-balanced among all running replicas.

  • 3️⃣ Updating Services With Zero Downtime

Docker Swarm supports rolling updates, allowing you to deploy new versions without downtime.

Updating Nginx To The Latest Version

docker service update --image nginx:latest nginx

Swarm gradually replaces old containers with new ones.

Ensures zero-downtime deployment by keeping some replicas online while updating others.

  • 4️⃣ Managing Service Health & Status

Swarm provides built-in commands for checking service health.

docker service logs nginx
  • 5️⃣ Rolling Back A Failed Deployment

If a new update causes issues, you can rollback to the previous version.

docker service rollback nginx

Reverts the service to the last known stable version.

Ensures minimal downtime and maintains application stability.

  • Final Steps & Next Actions

✅ Docker Swarm is now fully set up with running services.

✅ Applications can be scaled dynamically based on demand.

✅ Updates can be deployed with zero downtime using Swarm’s rolling updates.

Monitoring & Scaling Docker Swarm

Efficient monitoring and scaling are critical for maintaining a highly available and responsive Docker Swarm cluster.

✅ Setting up Prometheus & Grafana for real-time monitoring.

✅ Implementing auto-scaling for Swarm services based on system load.

  • 1️⃣ Setting Up Prometheus & Grafana For Monitoring

Prometheus is a powerful monitoring system that collects real-time metrics, while Grafana provides a visual dashboard for better observability.

🔹 Deploying Prometheus As A Swarm Service

Create a Prometheus configuration file (prometheus.yml) to scrape metrics from Swarm nodes.

global:  scrape_interval: 5s  - job_name: 'docker'      - targets: ['192.168.1.10:9323', '192.168.1.11:9323', '192.168.1.12:9323']

🔹 Deploy Prometheus As A Service

Run the following command to deploy Prometheus in Swarm -

docker service create --name prometheus \  --publish 9090:9090 \
  --mount type=bind,source=$(pwd)/prometheus.yml,target=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml \
  prom/prometheus

🔹 Deploying Grafana For Visualization

Grafana will provide a real-time dashboard for Swarm cluster metrics.

docker service create --name grafana \  --publish 3000:3000 \
  --env GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin \
  grafana/grafana
  • 2️⃣ Auto-Scaling Docker Swarm Services

Docker Swarm does not support native auto-scaling, but we can use Ansible and Prometheus alerts to dynamically adjust service replicas.

🔹 Scaling Services Based On CPU Usage

Create a Prometheus alert rule (alert.rules.yml) to detect high CPU usage -

groups:  - name: cpu_usage_alerts      - alert: HighCPUUsage
        expr: avg(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total[1m])) > 0.7
        for: 30s          severity: warning          description: "High CPU usage detected on Swarm nodes."

Deploy the alerting rule to Prometheus.

docker service update --mount-add type=bind,source=$(pwd)/alert.rules.yml,target=/etc/prometheus/alert.rules.yml prometheus

🔹 Ansible Playbook For Auto-Scaling Services

Create an Ansible playbook (scale_services.yml) to increase replicas dynamically.

- name: Auto-Scale Swarm Services  hosts: managers  tasks:      shell: "docker stats --no-stream --format '{{.CPUPerc}}' | awk '{s+=$1} END {print s/NR}'"
    - name: Scale Service if CPU > 70%
      command: "docker service scale nginx=10"
      when: cpu_load.stdout | float > 70

🔹 Automating Scaling With Cron Jobs

To run auto-scaling every minute, schedule the Ansible playbook using a cron job.

(crontab -l ; echo "* * * * * ansible-playbook scale_services.yml") | crontab -
  • Final Steps & Next Actions

✅ Prometheus & Grafana are deployed for real-time monitoring.

✅ Auto-scaling logic adjusts replicas based on CPU usage.

✅ Ansible automates scaling, ensuring efficient resource usage.

Common Pitfalls & Best Practices In Docker Swarm Automation

Automating a multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform & Ansible improves scalability, but there are common pitfalls that can impact security, performance, and reliability. Below is a breakdown of the most frequent issues and best practices to mitigate them.

  • 🛠 Best Practices For Scaling & Reliability

✅  — Use Terraform for provisioning, Ansible for configuration, and CI/CD pipelines for continuous deployments.

✅  — Implement IAM least privilege policies, TLS encryption, and secret management with HashiCorp Vault.

✅  — Monitor CPU, memory, and network usage with Prometheus, and auto-scale based on load.

✅  — Set up multi-region Swarm clusters and ensure backups of container data and logs.

✅  — Deploy new container versions gradually to prevent service disruptions.

By following these best practices, you can scale Docker Swarm efficiently while maintaining security and reliability. 🚀

Successfully deploying a highly available, multi-node Docker Swarm cluster requires a blend of infrastructure automation, configuration management, and best practices. Our DevOps teams at Xinthe leverage Terraform and Ansible to create a scalable, repeatable, and secure orchestration solution with minimal manual intervention.

By combining Terraform & Ansible for Docker Swarm automation we help our enterprise clientele build resilient, scalable, and cost-efficient container orchestration solutions. Following IaC best practices, implementing automated deployments, and optimizing monitoring, will now help you too streamline multi-node Swarm deployments while maintaining security, reliability, and operational efficiency.

items.title: Getting Started with Docker: A Beginner’s Guide
items.author: Pravesh Sudha
items.content:
Welcome to the world of DevOps! Today, we’re diving into an exciting tool that solves the classic software engineering problem:

“It worked on my machine, but not on yours!”

Yep, we’ve all been there. But thanks to , this headache is now a thing of the past!

In this blog, we’ll explore how Docker has revolutionized the DevOps industry, saving companies in computing costs. We’ll also get hands-on by creating a for a cloud-native monitoring application that tracks .

So, grab your coffee, and let’s get started! 🚀

💡 What is Docker? Understanding Containers & Why They Matter

Docker is a platform that allows us to develop, ship, and run applications in containers.

Wait… containers? Shipping? What’s going on here? 🚢Let’s slow down and break it down step by step.

Think of Docker as a tool that helps us create a of our application. Using this blueprint, we can run the application inside a .

But what exactly is a in Docker? 🤔A is like a lightweight operating system that includes everything needed to run an application — its dependencies, libraries, and configurations.

Here’s the of using Docker:

  1. We create a (this is the exact name it should have).
  2. Inside the , we define how our application should be built and run.
  3. Using some , we generate a  — this is the actual of our application.
  4. Finally, we use the to run a , where our application will actually execute.

Why Use Docker? Isn’t This More Complicated?

At first, Docker might seem complex, but here’s why it’s :

🔹 :Before Docker, we used to run applications on the cloud. However, VMs often , leading to . With Docker, we use that run on VMs, making applications much faster and more efficient.

🔹 Consistency Across Environments:When we create a , we choose a for our project (like Ubuntu, Alpine, or Python). This ensures that all developers and servers use the exact same environment, preventing the “works on my machine but not yours”issue.

🔹 Multiple Applications on One Machine:With Docker, we can run multiple applications on the same VM. Before Docker, we could usually run only one application per VM. This means better and .

Now that we understand , let’s move forward and build something cool with it! 🚀

💡 Installing Docker & Running Your First Container

Enough theory — let’s jump into action! 🏃💨

Step 1: Installing Docker

First, we need to install Docker on our system. Here’s how:

✅ : Download from Docker’s official website. It provides an easy-to-use GUI along with the Docker CLI.

✅ : You can install Docker using terminal commands. If you’re on , run:

sudo apt update -ysudo apt install docker.io -y
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER && newgrp docker
docker --version  # This will display the installed Docker version.

Once installed, Docker is ready to roll! 🎉

Step 2: Understanding DockerHub & Image Management

Earlier, we talked about 📝 helping us create 📦. But what if we the image we created? Our would be gone!

To avoid this, we have  — a cloud-based repository where we can store and share Docker images, just like .

  • : Open for everyone to use.
  • : Only accessible to authorized users.

This allows teams to collaborate and deploy applications consistently across different environments.

Step 3: Running Your First Docker Container

Let’s test Docker by running a :

docker run -d -p 8080:80 docker/welcome-to-docker
  • docker run → Runs a new container.
  • -d → Runs the container in (in the background).
  • -p 8080:80 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.
  • docker/welcome-to-docker → The we are running, which is pre-configured to display a welcome message.

Now, open your web browser and go to:

You should see a Docker Congratulations page! 🎉

This page is served by the image, which contains a simple web server (NGINX or Apache)displaying the welcome message.

Now that you’ve run your first Docker container, let’s move on to  🚀

💡 Building Our Own Docker Image: Cloud Monitoring App

Now that we’ve experimented with a demo Docker container, it’s time to build ! 🚀

We’ll use a Flask-based cloud monitoring application that displays of the machine it runs on.

Step 1: Cloning the Project Repository

First, let’s clone the project from GitHub:

git clone https://github.com/Pravesh-Sudha/cloud-native-monitoring-app.git

Next, navigate inside the project directory:

cd cloud-native-monitoring-app

Inside this directory, you’ll find a . Let’s break it down!

Step 2: Understanding the Dockerfile

Here’s the for our Flask-based cloud monitoring app:

# 1️⃣ Set the base imageFROM python:3.9-slim-buster  
# 2️⃣ Set the working directory inside the containerWORKDIR /app  
# 3️⃣ Copy the dependency file into the containerCOPY requirements.txt .  
# 4️⃣ Install dependenciesRUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt  
# 5️⃣ Copy the entire project into the containerCOPY . .  
# 6️⃣ Set environment variablesENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0  
# 7️⃣ Expose the application’s portEXPOSE 5000  
# 8️⃣ Define the command to run the Flask appCMD ["flask", "run"]

📌 FROM python:3.9-slim-buster → This sets our .

  • We’re using python:3.9-slim-buster, a version of Python 3.9.
  • Slim images are in size, making the container more efficient.

📌 WORKDIR /app → This sets the inside the container.

  • Just like we create a folder for our project, we define /app as the directory where our application code will live inside the container.

📌 COPY requirements.txt . → This copies requirements.txt into the container.

  • This file contains all the required for our Flask app.

📌 RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt → This installs the required .

📌 COPY . . → This copies our application files into the container.

📌 ENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 → This sets .

  • Here, we define FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 to allow our app to run properly inside a container or VM.

📌 EXPOSE 5000 → This exposes , the default port for Flask applications.

📌 CMD ["flask", "run"] → This defines the that will start our Flask app.

Step 3: Building the Docker Image

Now, let’s create a from this project. Run the following command:

docker build -t my-app:v1 .

🔹 -t my-app:v1 → Tags the image as with version v1.🔹 . → Tells Docker to use the as the build context.

Step 4: Running the Docker Container

Once the image is built successfully, let’s run it as a :

docker run -d -p 5000:5000 my-app:v1

🔹 -d → Runs the container in (in the background).🔹 -p 5000:5000 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.

Now, open your browser and go to:

🎉 You should see the application running!

Each time you refresh the page, the CPU and memory usage metrics will .

Now that we’ve successfully built and run our Dockerized cloud monitoring app.

Congratulations! 🎉 You’ve just taken your first steps into the world of Docker by learning how it solves real-world software deployment problems, running a demo container, and even Dockerizing a Flask-based cloud monitoring application.

Here’s a quick recap of what we covered:

✅ A platform for building, shipping, and running applications in lightweight containers.✅ It solves compatibility issues, improves resource utilization, and allows multiple applications to run efficiently on the same machine.✅ We installed Docker, ran a sample container, created a , built a , and launched a for our project.

This is just the beginning! Docker is a widely used in DevOps, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud deployments. The next step? Try experimenting with , push your image to , or deploy your containerized app to ! 🚀

If you found this blog helpful, feel free to share it with your fellow DevOps enthusiasts! Have questions? Drop them in the comments — I’d love to help. 😊

items.title: Freeipa/RedHat IdM API
items.author: Aditya Jambhalikar
items.content:
Red Hat Identity Management (IdM) provides a centralised and unified way to manage identity stores, authentication, policies, and authorisation policies in a Linux-based domain.

It also provides an option to use API (IPA API) to perform all the tasks programatically.

There are lots of ways to use it, but let’s check 3 popular ways which are curl , python and ansible

When it comes to quick use of API, we always think of using cURL. To use it for freeipa api, we first need to obtain cookies (create session) by logging in and then use the cookies for any further operation

The first thing to do is to define the environmental variables of the username, password, and cookies file.

export COOKIEJAR="/tmp/ipa" # path to store the cookie fileexport _USERNAME="admin" # or any other user with appropriate permission for the task you want to perform
 export _PASSWORD="<admin_password>" 
# Notice the whitespace before export, 
# It will make sure that the entry is not added to the termianl command history

The above step is optional, you can otherwise just pass the values directly in the request.

The next step is to login and obtain the cookies

curl -k  \-H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \
-H "Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
-H "Accept:text/plain" \
-c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \
--data "user=$_USERNAME&password=$_PASSWORD" \https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/login_password

If this is successful, then you can send any request

e.g. Let’s fetch all the users present in freeipa

curl -k \    -H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \
    -H "Content-Type:application/json" \
    -H "Accept:application/json"\
    -c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \
    -d  '{"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": true, "raw": true}]}' \    https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/json

The only part that will change in any request is the data-d

The Python way is similar as well. We create a session and then use it for any operation. There could be many ways to do it. But I prefer defining a class and using the session object.

import requestsclass IPA:
    def __init__(self, ipa_url):
        self.ipa_url = ipa_url
        self.session_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/json"
        self.referer = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa"
        self.session = requests.Session()
        self.req_header = {"Referer": self.referer, 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Accept': 'application/json'}
    def login(self, username="admin", password="default_admin_password"):
        data = {"user": username, "password": password}
        login_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/login_password"
        header = {"Referer": self.referer, "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded", "Accept": "text/plain"}
        res = self.session.post(login_url, headers=header, data=data, verify=False)
    def ipa_request(self, data):
        res = self.session.post(self.session_url, headers=self.req_header, data=json.dumps(data), verify=False)        data = {"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": True, "sizelimit": 0}]}
        res = self.ipa_request(data)
        return res
    def group_add_member(self, group_cn, users):
        data = {"method": "group_add_member", "params": [[f"{group_cn}"],
                                                         {"all": True, "user": users}]}
        print(f"\nrequest to send is:\n{data}")
        res = self.ipa_request(data)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    ipa = IPA(ipa_url="ipa.local.com")    users = ipa.user_find() 
    all_users = users["result"]["result"]
    print(all_users)
    res = ipa.group_add_member("test_group", ["user1", "user2"])

The above code will print all users present in freeipa and add the users user1 and user2 to the user group test_group

Can you modify the code and add the function to create the users and user groups?

Ansible is the best when it comes to configuring and managing different kinds of environments. The community general provides a module to configure the freeipa objects. It doesn’t support everything, it has most of the useful configuration objects

# vim: ft=yaml---
- name: Freeipa configuration
  hosts: localhost  environment:
    IPA_PASS: "<admin_password>" # This can be env var or vault var
    IPA_HOST: ipa.local.com    - "/root/hostgroup_list.yaml"    - name: Add hostgroups
      community.general.ipa_hostgroup:
        name: "{{ item.name }}"
        description: "{{ item.description }}"
        validate_certs: False
      with_items: "{{ hostgroup_list }}"
      tags: hostgroups

/root/hostgroup_list.yaml

hostgroup_list:  - description: 'test_group'

After this, you can run the playbook

ansible-playbook /root/ipa_playbook.yaml

This was just a simple example, but there are many better ways to do it

e.g. creating an inventory files, group_vars, encrypting IPA_PASS with ansible-vault etc

The freeipa API makes things easier and reduces operational overhead

Freeipa/RedHat IdM API was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: gRPC: For High-Performance Microservices Communication
items.author: DAMAN
items.content:
gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) is a high-performance, open-source RPC framework developed by Google. It enables efficient communication between services in microservices architectures by using Protocol Buffers (protobufs) for serialization and HTTP/2 as the transport protocol. Here’s a detailed breakdown of gRPC:

gRPC is a modern, high-performance framework for inter-service communication, supporting multiple programming languages. It is commonly used in to enable fast and efficient communication between backend services.

As applications grow in complexity — especially in microservices architectures — efficient communication between services becomes critical. Traditional REST-based APIs, while widely used, have performance and scalability limitations. gRPC solves these issues by offering faster, more efficient, and scalable communication

Uses HTTP/2 Instead of HTTP/1.1

  • Multiple requests can be sent in parallel over a single connection, reducing overhead.
  • Supports , where both client and server can send messages independently.
  • Reduces network bandwidth consumption.

Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) Instead of JSON

  • Binary serialization is more compact and faster than JSON.
  • Prevents data structure mismatches between client and server.
  • Backward and forward compatibility: Older clients can communicate with newer servers and vice versa.

Supports Multiple Languages

  • Official support for Go, Python, Java, Node.js, C++, C#, Ruby, PHP, etc.
  • Makes it easier to build (multi-language) microservices.

Authentication and Security

  • Supports (Transport Layer Security) for encryption.
  • Can integrate with OAuth, JWT, and API Gateway security models.

You define and using Protocol Buffers.

Generate Code for Client & Server

The .proto file is compiled using the protoc compiler to generate for the target language.

The server implements the defined RPC methods.

The client uses the generated stub to make remote procedure calls.

Unary RPC (Simple Request-Response)

  • Client sends a request → Server processes it → Server sends back a response.
  • Client sends a request → Server streams multiple responses back.
  • Client sends multiple requests → Server processes and sends a single response.

Bidirectional Streaming RPC

  • Both client and server send and receive multiple messages asynchronously.

✅ Microservices communication✅ High-performance APIs with low latency✅ Streaming data (video, chat, real-time updates)✅ Language-agnostic distributed systems✅ IoT and mobile apps (efficient data transfer)

❌ Many browsers don’t support gRPC natively (use REST or GraphQL instead).❌ Client-side apps that require direct browser support: gRPC-Web is needed for browser support.

Best Practices for gRPC in Kubernetes

  1. : Prefer Envoy or Nginx over default Kubernetes proxies
  2. Implement Client-Side Load Balancing: Distribute connection logic
  3. Leverage Service Mesh Technologies: Istio, Linkerd
  4. : Continuous performance evaluation
  5. Consider Alternative Deployment Strategies: StatefulSets, headless services

As cloud-native architectures continue to evolve, gRPC is positioning itself as a critical technology for building interconnected, efficient, and flexible distributed systems. With growing support from major tech companies and an active open-source community, gRPC is more than just a communication protocol — it’s a strategic approach to designing robust, performant microservices.

In the world of distributed computing, gRPC isn’t just about sending data — it’s about sending data , , and .

Whether you’re building the next generation of cloud services, IoT platforms, or complex distributed systems, gRPC offers a powerful toolkit for creating communication layers that are as dynamic and responsive as the applications they support.

I’ll be diving deep into and why Kubernetes isn’t always the best fit for gRPC — along with how to ! Stay tuned for the full blog.
