items.title: Creating a CI/CD Pipeline with GitHub Actions and Docker to Deploy on AWS Fargate
items.author: Nnyw
items.content:
In this tutorial, we’ll set up a continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipeline using to automate the deployment of a application packaged in a container to . This process will streamline our deployment workflow and enable efficient updates to our application.

Before diving into the setup, ensure you have the following:

  1. : A functional Spring Boot application that we want to deploy.
  2. : An active AWS account to manage services like ECR and ECS.
  3. : Installed locally to build our Docker image.
  4. : Installed and configured to interact with AWS services.
  5. : To host source code and manage CI/CD workflows.
  6. : An IAM user with permissions for Amazon ECS, ECR, and CloudWatch.

Setting Up AWS Environment

Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a managed container image registry that makes it easy for developers to store, manage, and deploy Docker container images. To start:

  1. Navigate to the ECR Dashboard: Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the ECR service.
  2. : Click on .
  3. : Enter a unique name for your repository (e.g., my-spring-boot-app).
  4. : Leave the default settings and click .

This repository will host the Docker images of your Spring Boot application, allowing ECS to pull the images for deployment.

Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) allows you to run Docker containers at scale. We will use Fargate, a serverless compute engine for containers:

  1. : In the AWS Management Console, navigate to ECS.
  2. : Click on and then .
  3. : Choose .
  4. : Provide a name for your cluster (e.g., production-cluster).
  5. : Click 

Our application will run in the . Using allows us to focus on building and running our application without managing the underlying server infrastructure.

Create ECS Task Definition

A is a blueprint for your application that specifies what containers to run and how to run them.

  1. Navigate to Task Definitions: In the ECS Dashboard, click on and then Create new Task Definition.
  2. : Choose as the launch type.
  3. : Fill in the task definition name and assign an execution role (you can create a new role with the necessary permissions).
  4. : Under the section, click :
  • : Enter my-spring-boot-app.
  • : Leave it blank for now; we will update this once we have pushed the Docker image to ECR.
  • : Set a memory limit (e.g., 512 MiB).
  • : Enter the port your Spring Boot application uses (commonly 8080).

5. Create Task Definition: Click .

The task definition serves as the configuration for our service, detailing which Docker image to use, resource allocations, and port mappings.

Ensure that our have the correct permissions. Attach the following to the user, allowing access to and :

{  "Version": "2012-10-17",    {      "Action": [        "ecs:DescribeServices",
        "ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition",
        "ecr:GetDownloadUrlForLayer",
        "ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability",
        "ecr:BatchGetImage",
        "ecr:GetAuthorizationToken",
        "ecr:PutImage",        "logs:PutLogEvents"      "Resource": "*"  ]

Dockerize Spring Boot Application

Dockerizing your application allows you to package your application with all its dependencies into a single container image that can be deployed anywhere.

: In the root of your Spring Boot project, create a file named Dockerfile:

# Use a base image with JDKFROM openjdk:11-jre-slim
# Set the working directory
WORKDIR /app
# Copy the jar file from the target folder to the working directory
COPY target/my-spring-boot-app.jar app.jar
# Expose the port your app runs on
EXPOSE 8080
# Command to run the application
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

Build the Docker Image Locally:

Before pushing to ECR, let’s build and test the Docker image locally to ensure everything is working:

./mvnw clean package  # Package your Spring Boot applicationdocker build -t my-spring-boot-app .

Run the Docker Image Locally:

After building, run the image to verify it behaves as expected:

docker run -p 8080:8080 my-spring-boot-app

This Dockerfile uses the OpenJDK slim image, copies the JAR file into the container, exposes the application port, and specifies how to run the application. Testing locally ensures that your application is functional before deployment.

After confirming that the Docker image works, the next step is to push this image to the ECR repository:

Authenticate Docker to ECR:

Use the AWS CLI to authenticate the Docker client to our ECR repository:

aws ecr get-login-password --region your-region | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin your-account-id.dkr.ecr.your-region.amazonaws.com

Tag the Docker image with the ECR repository URI:

docker tag my-spring-boot-app:latest your-account-id.dkr.ecr.your-region.amazonaws.com/my-spring-boot-app:latest

Push the Docker Image to ECR:

Finally, push the image to ECR:

docker push your-account-id.dkr.ecr.your-region.amazonaws.com/my-spring-boot-app:latest

Pushing the image to makes it accessible for to deploy our application. The tagging process ensures the image is correctly identified in the repository.

Configure GitHub Repository

Now that our application is containerized and pushed to ECR, we need to set up GitHub Actions to automate the deployment process.

  1. Create a GitHub Repository: If you haven’t already, create a new repository in GitHub and push your Spring Boot application code along with the Dockerfile.
  2. : For the GitHub Actions workflow to authenticate with AWS, we need to store sensitive information in GitHub Secrets:
  • Go to your GitHub repository > > > .
  • Click on and add the following secrets:
  • : Your AWS IAM user’s access key.
  • : Your AWS IAM user’s secret key.
  • : Your AWS region (e.g., ap-southeast-1).
  • : Your AWS account ID.
  • Using GitHub Secrets is crucial for maintaining the security of sensitive information. This prevents exposing credentials in your repository.

Create GitHub Actions Workflow

enables us to automate workflows for our projects directly from the GitHub repository. In this step, we will create a workflow that builds the image, pushes it to , and updates the depending on the branch of the code being pushed.

  1. Navigate to Your GitHub Repository: Open your GitHub repository where your Spring Boot project is hosted.
  2. Create a New Directory for Workflows: In your repository, create a new directory named .github/workflows. This is where GitHub looks for workflow definitions.
  3. : Inside the workflows directory, create a new file named ci-cd-pipeline.yml. This file will define the steps for your CI/CD process.

Now, let’s define the workflow in the ci-cd-pipeline.yml file. Below is an example of a workflow configuration that builds and deploys your Docker image based on the branch that is pushed:

name: Deploy to Amazon ECS  push:      - main    # Deploy to production when code is pushed to main
      - '*'     # Deploy to UAT for any other branch
env:
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
  AWS_REGION: ${{ secrets.AWS_REGION }}
  AWS_ACCOUNT_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}
  IMAGE_REPO_NAME_PROD: production-repo
  IMAGE_REPO_NAME_UAT: uat-repo
  ECS_CLUSTER_PROD: production-cluster
  ECS_CLUSTER_UAT: uat-cluster
  ECS_SERVICE_PROD: production-service
  ECS_SERVICE_UAT: uat-service  deploy:    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v2
      - name: Log in to Amazon ECR
        run: |
          aws ecr get-login-password --region ${{ env.AWS_REGION }} | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}.dkr.ecr.${{ env.AWS_REGION }}.amazonaws.com
      - name: Build the Docker image          docker build -t spring-boot-app:latest .
      - name: Tag the Docker image
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            docker tag spring-boot-app:latest ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}.dkr.ecr.${{ env.AWS_REGION }}.amazonaws.com/${{ env.IMAGE_REPO_NAME_PROD }}:latest            docker tag spring-boot-app:latest ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}.dkr.ecr.${{ env.AWS_REGION }}.amazonaws.com/${{ env.IMAGE_REPO_NAME_UAT }}:latest      - name: Push Docker image to Amazon ECR          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            docker push ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}.dkr.ecr.${{ env.AWS_REGION }}.amazonaws.com/${{ env.IMAGE_REPO_NAME_PROD }}:latest
          else
            docker push ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}.dkr.ecr.${{ env.AWS_REGION }}.amazonaws.com/${{ env.IMAGE_REPO_NAME_UAT }}:latest
          fi
      - name: Update ECS Service in Amazon ECS
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            aws ecs update-service --cluster ${{ env.ECS_CLUSTER_PROD }} --service ${{ env.ECS_SERVICE_PROD }} --force-new-deployment            aws ecs update-service --cluster ${{ env.ECS_CLUSTER_UAT }} --service ${{ env.ECS_SERVICE_UAT }} --force-new-deployment

: The ECS service is updated with the new image using the aws ecs update-service command, triggering a new deployment.

  • When code is pushed to the main branch, it deploys to .
  • For any other branch ('*' wildcard), it deploys to .
  1. : The actions/checkout action checks out the latest version of your code from GitHub.
  2. : This action sets up Docker to build and push multi-platform images.
  3. : The pipeline authenticates Docker to the AWS ECR registry using your credentials stored in GitHub Secrets.
  4. : The Spring Boot application is built into a Docker image using the docker build command.
  5. : Depending on the branch (either main for production or any other branch for UAT), the image is tagged with the appropriate ECR repository URL.
  6. Push Docker image to Amazon ECR: The tagged Docker image is pushed to the corresponding ECR repository (either production or UAT).

Commit and Push Your Changes

Now that your workflow is set up, commit the changes to the .github/workflows/ci-cd-pipeline.yml file:

git add .github/workflows/ci-cd-pipeline.ymlgit commit -m "Add CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions"

When you push changes to the main or other branches, the workflow will automatically run. You can monitor the progress of the workflow by navigating to the tab in your GitHub repository.

In this tutorial, we’ve walked through the process of creating a CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions to deploy a Spring Boot application on AWS Fargate. By following this guide, you’ve set up to automate the deployment of your Spring Boot application to .

This workflow ensures that whenever you push to the main branch, your application is automatically deployed to the production environment, while other branches trigger a deployment to UAT. This CI/CD approach helps streamline your deployments and improve the consistency of your release process.

items.title: Microservices Design Pattern: Explain Saga Pattern with Example(Part 2)
items.author: Aayushi Chourasiya
items.content:
The is used in to manage distributed transactions.

Two Types of Saga Patterns:

  1. (Event-Driven)
  2. (Centralized Control)

Example: Ordering a Product in an E-Commerce System

Imagine an online shopping system with these three microservices:

  1. → Creates an order
  2. → Deducts money
  3. → Reserves stock

1. Choreography-Based Saga (Event-Driven)

  • Each service listens for and responds independently.
  • Services communicate using .
  • Rollback happens indirectly based on failure scenarios.

Step 1: Order Service (Emits ORDER_CREATED Event)

@RestController@RequestMapping("/orders")
public class OrderController {
    @Autowired private KafkaTemplate<String, String> kafkaTemplate;    public ResponseEntity<String> createOrder(@RequestBody OrderRequest request) {
        // Save order logic (omitted)
        kafkaTemplate.send("order-topic", "ORDER_CREATED:" + request.getOrderId());
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Order placed successfully!");
    }
  • A REST endpoint /orders allows users to place an order.
  • It an event "ORDER_CREATED:<orderId>" to Kafka .
  • Other microservices listen for this event to continue the process.

Step 2: Payment Service (Listens for ORDER_CREATED and Emits PAYMENT_SUCCESS/FAILED)

@Component@KafkaListener(topics = "order-topic", groupId = "payment-group")
public class PaymentListener {
    @Autowired private KafkaTemplate<String, String> kafkaTemplate;
    public void processOrder(String message) {
        if (message.startsWith("ORDER_CREATED")) {
            String orderId = message.split(":")[1];
            // Simulate payment processing
            if (Math.random() > 0.2) { // 80% success rate
                kafkaTemplate.send("payment-topic", "PAYMENT_SUCCESS:" + orderId);                kafkaTemplate.send("payment-topic", "PAYMENT_FAILED:" + orderId);        }}
  • This service listens for "ORDER_CREATED" events from Kafka.
  • It extracts the orderId from the event.
  • It simulates (80% chance of success).
  • If payment is , it emits "PAYMENT_SUCCESS:<orderId>" to Kafka.
  • If , it emits "PAYMENT_FAILED:<orderId>" , and the order will be canceled.

Step 3: Inventory Service (Listens for PAYMENT_SUCCESS and Emits STOCK_RESERVED/STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE)

@Component@KafkaListener(topics = "payment-topic", groupId = "inventory-group")
public class InventoryListener {
    @Autowired private KafkaTemplate<String, String> kafkaTemplate;
    public void processPayment(String message) {
        if (message.startsWith("PAYMENT_SUCCESS")) {
            String orderId = message.split(":")[1];
            // Simulate inventory check
            if (Math.random() > 0.1) { // 90% success rate
                kafkaTemplate.send("inventory-topic", "STOCK_RESERVED:" + orderId);                kafkaTemplate.send("inventory-topic", "STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE:" + orderId);        } else if (message.startsWith("PAYMENT_FAILED")) {
            System.out.println("Payment failed, order is canceled.");
        }}
  • This service listens for "PAYMENT_SUCCESS" events.
  • It simulates (90% chance stock is available).
  • If stock is available, it emits "STOCK_RESERVED:<orderId>".
  • If stock is unavailable, it emits "STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE:<orderId>", triggering a .

Step 4: Order Service (Listens for STOCK_RESERVED or STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE)

@Component@KafkaListener(topics = "inventory-topic", groupId = "order-group")
public class OrderListener {
    public void processInventory(String message) {
        if (message.startsWith("STOCK_RESERVED")) {
            System.out.println("Order completed successfully!");
        } else if (message.startsWith("STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE")) {
            System.out.println("Stock not available, refunding payment.");    }
  • Listens for "STOCK_RESERVED" (order is successful 🎉).
  • Listens for "STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE" (order is , and payment is ).

How Rollback Happens in Choreography?

  • If , "PAYMENT_FAILED" event cancels the order.
  • If , "STOCK_NOT_AVAILABLE" the event .
  • No centralized rollback, each service reacts to events independently.

2. Orchestration-Based Saga (Centralized Control)

Here, a service controls the process:

  • Calls each service using REST API.
  • If any step , the orchestrator triggers a .

Step 1: Saga Orchestrator (Controls Workflow)

@Servicepublic class OrderSagaOrchestrator {
    @Autowired private RestTemplate restTemplate;
    public String startSaga(String orderId) {        ResponseEntity<String> orderResponse = restTemplate.postForEntity(
            "http://localhost:8081/orders", orderId, String.class);
        if (!orderResponse.getStatusCode().is2xxSuccessful()) {
            return "Order creation failed!";        // Step 2: Process Payment
        ResponseEntity<String> paymentResponse = restTemplate.postForEntity(
            "http://localhost:8082/payments", orderId, String.class);
        if (!paymentResponse.getStatusCode().is2xxSuccessful()) {
            // Rollback Order
            restTemplate.delete("http://localhost:8081/orders/" + orderId);
            return "Payment failed, order canceled!";        // Step 3: Reserve Stock
        ResponseEntity<String> stockResponse = restTemplate.postForEntity(
            "http://localhost:8083/inventory", orderId, String.class);
        if (!stockResponse.getStatusCode().is2xxSuccessful()) {
            // Rollback Payment
            restTemplate.delete("http://localhost:8082/payments/" + orderId);
            restTemplate.delete("http://localhost:8081/orders/" + orderId);
            return "Stock unavailable, order & payment rolled back!";
        }
        return "Order successfully completed!";
    }
  • Calls Order Service to create an order.
  • Calls Payment Service to process payment.
  • Calls Inventory Service to reserve stock.
  • If , it cancels the order.
  • If , it and .
@RestController@RequestMapping("/orders")
public class OrderController {
    private final Map<String, String> orders = new HashMap<>();    public ResponseEntity<String> createOrder(@RequestBody String orderId) {
        orders.put(orderId, "CREATED");
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Order Created");    @DeleteMapping("/{orderId}")
    public ResponseEntity<String> cancelOrder(@PathVariable String orderId) {
        orders.remove(orderId);
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Order Canceled");
    }
  • Creates an order and stores it in a HashMap.
  • If the orchestrator calls the DELETE API, it .
@RestController@RequestMapping("/payments")
public class PaymentController {
    private final Map<String, String> payments = new HashMap<>();    public ResponseEntity<String> processPayment(@RequestBody String orderId) {
        payments.put(orderId, "PAID");
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Payment Processed");    @DeleteMapping("/{orderId}")
    public ResponseEntity<String> refundPayment(@PathVariable String orderId) {
        payments.remove(orderId);
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Payment Refunded");
    }
  • If payment succeeds, it stores PAID status.
  • If a , it the payment.

Summary: When to Use What?

Both and Saga patterns help manage transactions in microservices. is event-driven and decentralized but can get complex, while offers better control with a central coordinator.

Choosing the right approach depends on your system’s complexity, scalability needs, and failure handling strategy. Pick wisely to build resilient microservices! 🚀

Which approach would you choose? Let’s discuss!

items.title: Scaling AWS Organizations For Large Enterprises — Key Trade-Offs & Best Practices
items.author: Noel
items.content:
Scaling AWS Organizations For Large Enterprises — Key Trade-Offs & Best Practices

For enterprises with 1000+ AWS accounts, the AWS Organizations model introduces hard limits on Service Control Policies (SCPs), permission boundaries, and character constraints. As a result, organizations must balance -

🚨  — Keeping security and compliance manageable while allowing flexibility for different teams.

🚨  — AWS imposes limits on SCPs, number of accounts per organization, and IAM policies.

🚨 Developer Autonomy vs. Centralized Control — Teams need freedom to innovate, but centralized governance must ensure security and compliance.

At this scale, AWS’s default recommendation of a single organization may no longer be practical, and enterprises must evaluate when to transition to a multi-org strategy.

By the end of this guide, you’ll have a clear understanding of how our AWS specialists at Xinthe go about setting and implementing -

📌 AWS Organization limitations and workarounds for scaling.

📌 Advanced governance techniques for managing 1000+ AWS accounts.

📌 Strategies used by large enterprises to manage multiple AWS Organizations effectively.

Understanding AWS Organizations & Multi-Account Strategy

  • What Is AWS Organizations?

AWS Organizations is a centralized governance and account management service that enables enterprises to -

✔ Group AWS accounts hierarchically using Organizational Units (OUs).

✔ Enforce policies using Service Control Policies (SCPs).

✔ Manage billing centrally through Consolidated Billing.

✔ Integrate with AWS Control Tower for automated landing zone setup.

✔ Create cross-account IAM roles for secure access management.

Key AWS Organizations Features

While AWS recommends consolidating accounts under a single AWS Organization, scaling challenges emerge when enterprises exceed 1000+ accounts, requiring a deeper evaluation of governance models.

  • Benefits of a Multi-Account Strategy

A multi-account AWS strategy is essential for large enterprises, enabling -

Minimizes the blast radius by separating environments (e.g., production, staging, dev).

Prevents privilege escalation — even if one account is compromised, the damage is contained.

Supports regulatory compliance (e.g., PCI-DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP) by enforcing isolation.

2️⃣ Billing & Cost Allocation

Each AWS account represents a billing unit, enabling cost tracking by team, business unit, or project.

AWS Cost Explorer & AWS Budgets provide granular insights into spend distribution.

Organizations can apply chargeback models, ensuring teams are accountable for their cloud consumption.

3️⃣ Resource Limits & Performance Isolation

AWS enforces account-level service quotas (e.g., EC2 instances per region, Lambda concurrency).

Multiple accounts prevent quota exhaustion — if one account hits a limit, others remain unaffected.

Dedicated accounts for high-performance workloads improve reliability and fault tolerance.

4️⃣ Compliance & Governance

Industry regulations (GDPR, SOC 2, HIPAA) often require account isolation for compliance.

Centralized governance with AWS Organizations, AWS Config, and AWS Security Hub ensures adherence to security policies.

  • Common Multi-Account Architectures

1️⃣ Traditional Model — Single AWS Account With Multiple IAM Roles (Not Scalable)

IAM policies grow complex, increasing security risks.

Resource limits (service quotas) constrain scalability.

No clear cost segregation, making it difficult to track team-level cloud spend.

2️⃣ Enterprise Model — Multi-Account Structure With Centralized Governance

✅ Best for: Large enterprises with 1000+ AWS accounts.

✅ Uses AWS Organizations with hierarchical OUs -

(audit, logging, monitoring).

(networking, shared services).

(e.g., Dev, Test, Prod).

📌 Example Multi-Account AWS Organization Architecture:

Root AWS Organization  │── Security OU  │   ├── Audit Account  │   ├── Networking Account  
│   ├── Shared Services Account  
│── Business Units OU  │   ├── Prod Accounts

While AWS recommends a single AWS Organization, enterprises with 1000+ accounts may need to evaluate the need for multiple Organizations to bypass policy limitations and enhance scalability.

Designing A Scalable AWS Organizations Structure

Managing 1000+ AWS accounts requires a well-defined AWS Organizations structure that balances security, scalability, and operational efficiency. A poorly structured AWS Organization leads to governance challenges, policy misconfigurations, and unnecessary complexity.

Below, we explore the recommended multi-account hierarchy, best practices for Organizational Units (OUs), and how to enforce governance using Service Control Policies (SCPs).

  • 1️⃣ Recommended AWS Account Hierarchy

A hierarchical account structure ensures proper separation of duties, security isolation, and operational scalability.

🚀 AWS Organizations Account Design (Recommended Model)

✔ Minimizes the blast radius of security incidents by isolating key functions.

✔ Prevents privilege escalation by separating security, networking, and workloads.

✔ Enables granular cost allocation by assigning billing per business unit.

  • 2️⃣ Organizational Units (OUs) Best Practices

🔹 How OUs Improve AWS Governance

OUs group related accounts together, allowing administrators to apply SCPs, IAM policies, and AWS Config rules consistently.

📌 Recommended AWS OU Design

✔ Do not mix different security levels within the same OU. Keep production workloads separate from development accounts.

✔ Apply SCPs at the OU level to enforce security standards across multiple accounts.

✔ Group accounts by business function, not by individual teams — teams change over time, but business units remain stable.

  • 3️⃣ Service Control Policies (SCPs) For Governance

SCPs act as “guardrails” for AWS Organizations, restricting actions that accounts within an OU or the entire organization can perform.

📌 Example SCP Policies For A Secure AWS Organization

✔ Apply restrictive policies at the OU level to ensure fine-grained control over different environments.

✔ Use SCPs alongside IAM permissions — SCPs define what’s denied globally, IAM defines what’s allowed per role.

  • Final Thoughts On AWS Organization Structure

By following these best practices -

✔ You ensure strong security isolation across AWS accounts.

✔ You improve scalability by defining clear Organizational Units (OUs).

✔ You enforce governance using SCPs to prevent misconfigurations.

Security & Compliance At Scale

Scaling AWS security and compliance across 1000+ accounts requires a centralized governance approach to logging, identity management, and policy enforcement. A fragmented security model increases attack surfaces, misconfiguration risks, and compliance violations.

In this section, we outline our best practices for managing security & compliance at scale using AWS native services, automation, and policy-based enforcement.

  • 1️⃣ Centralized Security & Logging

🔹 Why Centralized Logging Matters?

Without centralized log aggregation, security teams struggle to detect and respond to security threats across multiple AWS accounts. A dedicated Logging Account ensures that logs cannot be tampered with while providing organization-wide visibility.

📌 Best Practices For Centralized Logging

💡 Store CloudTrail logs in an S3 bucket with write-only permissions to prevent log tampering.

  • 2️⃣ IAM Best Practices For Multi-Account Environments

🔹 Why IAM Centralization is Critical?

Poor identity and access management (IAM) practices lead to privilege escalation, excessive permissions, and security breaches. At scale, manual IAM management becomes unsustainable, requiring automation and centralized controls.

📌 IAM Security Best Practices

💡 Enforce IAM security best practices via AWS Organizations SCPs to prevent human error and privilege escalation

  • 3️⃣ Automated Compliance & Policy Enforcement

🔹 Why Automate Compliance?

With 1000+ AWS accounts, manually ensuring compliance with CIS, NIST, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR standards is impossible. Automating compliance reduces operational overhead while ensuring continuous monitoring and remediation.

📌 AWS Compliance Automation Framework

💡 Implement self-healing security automation using AWS Lambda + EventBridge to detect and remediate policy violations in real-time.

  • Final Thoughts On AWS Security & Compliance At Scale

By implementing these best practices, enterprises can -

✔ Enforce security guardrails across AWS accounts without manual intervention.

✔ Ensure compliance with regulatory standards through automation.

✔ Reduce security risks by centralizing IAM, logging, and threat detection.

Automation & CI/CD In Multi-Account AWS Environments

Managing 1000+ AWS accounts at scale requires automation-first strategies to ensure consistency, security, and operational efficiency. Manually provisioning resources, enforcing policies, and managing security baselines across multiple AWS accounts is not sustainable.

This section covers best practices for Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC), automated AWS account provisioning, and implementing CI/CD pipelines to manage AWS Organizations at scale.

  • 1️⃣ Infrastructure-As-Code (IaC) For AWS Account Management

🔹 Why Use IaC For Multi-Account Environments?

Standardizes infrastructure provisioning across accounts.

Reduces human error and misconfigurations.

Ensures compliance by enforcing security baselines via automated deployments.

📌 Recommended IaC Tools For AWS Organizations

📌 Automating VPC, IAM, & Security Policies Across Accounts

✅ Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to provision VPCs, IAM roles, and security policies at the OU level.

✅ Enforce consistent network configurations across accounts (e.g., centralized VPC peering, security groups).

✅ Automate IAM policies and role creation to ensure least-privilege access across accounts.

💡 Store Terraform state files in AWS S3 with DynamoDB locking for secure state management.

  • 2️⃣ AWS Control Tower For Automated Account Provisioning

🔹 Why Use AWS Control Tower?

AWS Control Tower automates AWS account creation and governance by applying best practices, pre-configured security controls, and policy enforcement from day one.

📌 AWS Control Tower Features

📌 How To Use AWS Control Tower For Multi-Account AWS Management

✅ Deploy Landing Zones to enforce standardized IAM, VPC, and security policies across accounts.

✅ Automate AWS account creation using AWS Control Tower Account Factory.

✅ Use Guardrails (SCPs & AWS Config Rules) to enforce security compliance.

💡 Integrate AWS Control Tower with Terraform to extend governance beyond AWS-native features.

  • 3️⃣ CI/CD Pipeline For AWS Account Deployments

🔹 Why Use CI/CD For AWS Organizations?

Automates infrastructure deployments across multiple AWS accounts.

Enforces security policies and SCPs consistently.

Enables GitOps workflows for AWS infrastructure management.

📌 Recommended CI/CD Workflow For AWS Organizations

📌 Example — Automating SCP Deployment Using Terraform & AWS Lambda

Step 1 — Define SCPs In Terraform

Key Best Practices for Cost Monitoring and Allocatioresource "aws_organizations_policy" "deny_s3_public_access" {  name        = "DenyS3PublicAccess"
  description = "Prevents public access to S3 buckets."
  type        = "SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY"{  "Statement": [      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "s3:PutBucketPolicy",
      "Resource": "*",        "StringNotEquals": {
          "aws:PrincipalOrgID": "o-xxxxxxxxxx"
        }    }}}

Step 2 — Deploy SCPs To AWS Organization Using Lambda

import boto3    client = boto3.client("organizations")
    response = client.attach_policy(
        PolicyId="p-1234567890",    )

Step 3 — Automate Execution Via AWS CodePipeline

stages:  - stage: Validate      - job: Terraform_Validate          - run: terraform fmt -check
          - run: terraform validate
    jobs:        steps:
          - run: terraform apply -auto-approve

📌 Key Benefits Of CI/CD For AWS Multi-Account Deployments

✅ Standardized security policies & IAM configurations across all AWS accounts.

✅ Automated enforcement of AWS governance best practices.

✅ Faster AWS account provisioning & onboarding.

💡 Use GitOps workflows to track AWS configuration changes, ensuring version-controlled infrastructure deployments.

  • Final Thoughts On AWS Automation & CI/CD At Scale

By implementing Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC), AWS Control Tower, and CI/CD pipelines, enterprises can -

✔ Automate AWS account provisioning, reducing operational overhead.

✔ Enforce security & compliance policies at scale using SCPs & AWS Config.

✔ Ensure consistency across AWS accounts, minimizing misconfigurations.

Cost Management & Budgeting In AWS Organizations

Efficient cost management is a critical aspect of running large-scale AWS Organizations. With hundreds or even thousands of AWS accounts, maintaining control over spending, allocating costs accurately, and optimizing cloud resources becomes challenging. Below, you’ll find best practices for consolidated billing, cost allocation, budgeting, and automated cost control in multi-account AWS environments.

  • 1️⃣ Consolidated Billing & Cost Allocation Best Practices

Why Consolidated Billing Matters?

Consolidated billing allows enterprises to centralize cost management, gain visibility into spending across multiple AWS accounts, and optimize resource usage. It simplifies cost reporting and enables accurate chargebacks to different teams or business units.

Key Best Practices For Cost Monitoring & Allocation:

Example Tagging Strategy:

💡 Enforce mandatory tagging across accounts using AWS Organizations Service Control Policies (SCPs) to ensure cost visibility and accurate chargebacks.

  • 2️⃣ Implementing AWS Savings Plans & Reserved Instances At Scale

Why Use Savings Plans & Reserved Instances (RIs)?

AWS Savings Plans and Reserved Instances offer significant cost savings for predictable workloads by providing discounted rates for long-term commitments. In multi-account setups, sharing these plans across accounts maximizes cost efficiency.

Strategies For Optimizing Savings Plans & RIs:

aws ce get-reservation-utilization --time-period Start=2023-01-01,End=2023-01-31

This command provides detailed RI utilization data to help identify underutilized reservations.

💡 Regularly review AWS Cost Explorer and Compute Optimizer recommendations to adjust Savings Plans and RIs based on changing workload patterns.

  • 3️⃣ Cost Guardrails & Automated Budget Enforcement

Proactive Cost Management With AWS Budgets & Alerts:

AWS Budgets allow you to set thresholds for cost, usage, and reservation coverage. Automated alerts ensure teams are notified of unexpected cost spikes or anomalies.

Automated Budget Enforcement With AWS Lambda:

Use AWS Lambda functions to take automated actions when budgets are exceeded, such as restricting services or scaling down resources.

Example AWS Budgets Setup:

aws budgets create-budget \  --account-id 123456789012 \
  --budget '{"BudgetName":"MonthlyCostBudget","BudgetLimit":{"Amount":"10000","Unit":"USD"},"TimeUnit":"MONTHLY","BudgetType":"COST"}'

Example Lambda Function For Cost Control:

import boto3import json
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    cost_exceeded = event['detail']['cost_exceeded']        ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
        # Stop all non-critical instances
        response = ec2.stop_instances(InstanceIds=['i-0abcd1234', 'i-0efgh5678'])
        print("Stopped instances due to budget limit exceeded:", response)

Using AWS Budgets Actions:

AWS Budgets Actions can automatically trigger Lambda functions when budget thresholds are breached, allowing for real-time cost control.

Real-Time Cost Visibility:

💡 Integrate AWS Budgets with Amazon SNS for real-time alerts and use AWS Lambda for automated cost remediation.

  • Final Thoughts On Cost Management In AWS Organizations

By adopting these cost management practices -

✔ Gain visibility into cloud spending across all AWS accounts.

✔ Optimize costs using Savings Plans, RIs, and Compute Optimizer.

✔ Enforce cost guardrails with automated budgeting and real-time alerts.

Common Pitfalls & Trade-Offs In Scaling AWS Organizations

Scaling AWS Organizations across hundreds or thousands of accounts presents technical, operational, and security challenges. While AWS Organizations provides a robust framework for managing multi-account environments, misconfigurations, poor governance, and excessive decentralization can lead to inefficiencies and security risks.

As part of this section, we go over common pitfalls and trade-offs in structuring AWS Organizations at scale, along with best practices to balance governance, security, and operational agility.

  • 1️⃣ Overly Centralized Vs. Fully Decentralized Models

🔹 The Trade-Off Between Governance & Developer Agility

AWS Organizations can be structured in three primary governance models -

: All infrastructure, security, and cost governance decisions are made by a central cloud team.

: Individual teams have complete autonomy over their AWS accounts.

Hybrid Model (Recommended): A balance of centralized governance with decentralized team autonomy, enabled through automation and policy enforcement.

💡 Implement centralized security, networking, and billing governance while giving application teams controlled autonomy over infrastructure.

  • 2️⃣ Poor Permission Management

🔹 Why Excessive IAM Permissions Lead To Security Risks?

One of the most common security pitfalls in large AWS Organizations is over-permissive IAM policies. When permissions are too broad, unauthorized access and privilege escalation become serious threats.

📌 Best Practices For IAM Security In Multi-Account AWS Environments

💡 Use Service Control Policies (SCPs) to block overly permissive IAM actions and enforce least privilege across all AWS accounts.

  • 3️⃣ Complexity In Multi-Region & Multi-Cloud AWS Setups

🔹 Multi-Region AWS Challenges

As AWS Organizations scale globally, enterprises face compliance, latency, and data residency challenges when deploying workloads across multiple AWS regions.

📌 Multi-Region Best Practices

💡 Use AWS Local Zones & Outposts for low-latency deployments in specific geographic locations.

🔹 Multi-Cloud AWS Challenges

Some enterprises run AWS alongside Azure, GCP, or on-prem environments, leading to interoperability and connectivity challenges.

📌 Multi-Cloud Best Practices

💡 Use Kubernetes (EKS, AKS, GKE) for portable, multi-cloud application deployments.

  • Final Thoughts On Avoiding Pitfalls In AWS Organizations

By implementing these best practices -

✔ Balance governance with team autonomy using a hybrid AWS Organizations model.

✔ Enforce least-privilege IAM access to reduce security risks across AWS accounts.

✔ Optimize multi-region and multi-cloud architectures for scalability, compliance, and cost efficiency.

Future Trends In Scaling AWS Organizations

As AWS Organizations continue to evolve, enterprises must adapt to emerging trends in security, automation, and cost optimization. The future of multi-account AWS governance will be driven by AI-driven automation, serverless identity management, zero-trust architectures, and multi-cloud enterprise strategies. Explored below are key trends shaping the future of AWS Organizations at scale.

  • 1️⃣ AI & ML For Automated Governance

🔹 AI-Driven Security Anomaly Detection In AWS

With the growing complexity of AWS environments, AI-powered threat detection is becoming essential for identifying security anomalies and unauthorized access patterns in AWS Organizations.

📌 AI-Based Security Best Practices

💡 AI-powered continuous compliance auditing will become standard practice in AWS Organizations.

🔹 Predictive Cost Optimization With Machine Learning Models

Managing cloud costs at scale is challenging. AI-powered cost analysis is becoming a key trend for real-time cost prediction and optimization.

📌 AI-Powered Cost Optimization Strategies

💡 AI will automate cost-saving actions, such as auto-scaling compute resources based on predicted usage.

  • 2️⃣ Serverless & Zero Trust Architectures

🔹 Serverless Identity Management With AWS Cognito & Identity Federation

As enterprises scale AWS Organizations, managing millions of IAM users and roles manually becomes impractical. Serverless identity management via AWS Cognito and federated authentication will streamline access control.

📌 Serverless Identity Management Best Practices

💡 AWS will further integrate Cognito with AWS Organizations to enable centralized authentication at scale.

🔹 Zero-Trust IAM Policies Across AWS Organizations

Traditional network-based security models are becoming obsolete. Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) enforces identity-first security policies, ensuring that every access request is authenticated and continuously verified.

📌 Implementing Zero Trust In AWS Organizations

💡 AWS will integrate zero-trust network policies with IAM for adaptive security enforcement.

  • 3️⃣ Multi-Cloud Management In Enterprise AWS Setups

🔹 Using AWS Organizations With Hybrid AWS & Azure Environments

As enterprise IT landscapes evolve, many organizations leverage multiple cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) for resiliency and compliance. AWS Organizations must support multi-cloud governance models.

📌 Best Practices For Multi-Cloud AWS Governance

💡 AWS may expand AWS Organizations to support cross-cloud policies for hybrid cloud governance.

  • Final Thoughts On The Future Of AWS Organizations

🔹 AI-driven automation will enhance security, compliance, and cost control in AWS Organizations.

🔹 Serverless identity management and zero-trust security will redefine IAM governance at scale.

🔹 Multi-cloud AWS governance will become a priority as enterprises expand across multiple cloud providers.

Managing AWS Organizations at scale is a complex task that requires governance, security enforcement, automation, and cost control. As enterprises grow, balancing centralized policies with developer autonomy becomes crucial for efficiency, security, and agility.

Scaling AWS Organizations in large enterprises isn’t simply about creating accounts — it requires a well-defined strategy that integrates - ✔ Security & Compliance Controls

✔ Automation & Infrastructure-as-Code

✔ Cost Governance & Predictive Optimization

✔ AI-driven Monitoring & Multi-Cloud Readiness

Through the implemention of structured governance, automation, and financial accountability, our AWS specialists at Xinthe have helped our client organizations scale their AWS environments securely, cost-efficiently, and with minimal operational overhead. Armed with the knowledge shared in this article, now you can too!

items.title: Leveraging RabbitMQ for IoT: Efficient Message Queuing for Scalable IoT Solutions
items.author: Andrew Karanja
items.content:
The has transformed the way devices communicate, collect, and process data. From to automated industrial systems, IoT devices generate massive amounts of real-time data that needs to be processed, analyzed, and acted upon efficiently.

However, handling high-frequency data streams from thousands (or even millions) of IoT devices presents major challenges:

  •  — How do you process millions of messages without overwhelming the system?
  •  — What happens if a server fails? Will messages be lost?
  •  — How do you ensure near real-time processing?
  •  — How do you send data to the right services for processing?

This is where , a high-performance message broker, plays a crucial role in .

In this article, I will share for an IoT-based smart metering project, , and the along the way.

RabbitMQ is an open-source message broker that enables efficient asynchronous communication between devices, services, and applications. It supports multiple messaging patterns and ensures message reliability, scalability, and delivery guarantees.

Key Features of RabbitMQ in IoT

✅ Ensures messages are delivered even if the receiver is temporarily unavailable.✅ Supports clustering and high availability for large-scale IoT deployments.✅ Processes thousands of messages per second, enabling real-time data handling.✅ Uses exchanges, queues, and bindings to direct messages dynamically.✅ Persistence & Acknowledgment: Ensures messages are not lost even if the broker crashes.✅ Works with MQTT, AMQP, WebSockets, and more.

These features make RabbitMQ a for managing efficiently.

When implementing a smart water and power metering solution, I had to process and analyze millions of IoT messages daily. The goal was to: 1️⃣ Collect real-time water & power consumption from smart meters.2️⃣ Process the data and store it in a database for analytics & billing.3️⃣ Route alerts to different based on device status.4️⃣ Ensure messages even during network failures.

After evaluating different solutions like Kafka, MQTT brokers, and Redis Streams, I settled on due to its simplicity, robustness, and flexibility.

🚀 Why I Implemented It This Way:

  • Lightweight & Easy to Deploy — Unlike Kafka, which requires , RabbitMQ is .
  • Efficient for IoT Workloads — Handles millions of small messages efficiently.
  •  — I could dynamically based on and .
  •  — Messages if the system crashes.
  •  — It supports to distribute workload across multiple servers.

IoT Architecture with RabbitMQ

How Data Flows in an IoT System

To understand how RabbitMQ fits into an IoT ecosystem, let’s break it down:

1️⃣ IoT Devices (Sensors & Meters) → Collect real-time data2️⃣ → Sends the data to RabbitMQ3️⃣ RabbitMQ Exchanges & Queues → Routes the data to different consumers4️⃣ Consumers (Microservices & Databases) → Process, store, and act on the data

  • Smart meters publish readings (e.g., water consumption, power usage).
  • RabbitMQ receives and queues the messages.
  • Multiple microservices subscribe to different message queues:
  • → Calculates customer bills.
  • → Detects meter failures or leaks.
  • → Notifies users in case of anomalies.
  • → Saves data into MongoDB for analytics.

RabbitMQ Implementation: Setting Up an IoT Message Broker

On an , install RabbitMQ using:

sudo apt updatesudo apt install rabbitmq-server -y
sudo systemctl enable rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl start rabbitmq-server

Enable the RabbitMQ to monitor queues:

sudo rabbitmq-plugins enable rabbitmq_management

Now, you can access the at:http://your-server-ip:15672/ (Default login: guest/guest)

Step 2: Defining Exchanges & Queues

RabbitMQ uses to route messages to queues.

  • Routes data to the right queue based on device type.
  • water-meters → Handles smart water meter data
  • power-meters → Handles smart power meter data
  • alerts → Handles system alerts for anomalies

Using RabbitMQ CLI, create an and queues:

rabbitmqctl add_vhost /iotrabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /iot guest ".*" ".*" ".*"
rabbitmqadmin declare exchange name=iot.direct type=directrabbitmqadmin declare queue name=water-meters durable=true
rabbitmqadmin declare queue name=power-meters durable=true
rabbitmqadmin declare queue name=alerts durable=true
rabbitmqadmin declare binding source=iot.direct destination=water-meters routing_key=water
rabbitmqadmin declare binding source=iot.direct destination=power-meters routing_key=power
rabbitmqadmin declare binding source=iot.direct destination=alerts routing_key=alert

Step 3: Publishing Data from IoT Devices

Each publishes data using MQTT or AMQP.

Example :

const amqp = require('amqplib');
async function publishReading(meterId, value, type) {    const connection = await amqp.connect('amqp://localhost');
    const channel = await connection.createChannel();
    const exchange = 'iot.direct';
    const routingKey = type === 'water' ? 'water' : 'power';
    const message = JSON.stringify({ meterId, value, timestamp: new Date() });
    await channel.assertExchange(exchange, 'direct', { durable: true });
    channel.publish(exchange, routingKey, Buffer.from(message));
    console.log(`Sent: ${message}`);
    setTimeout(() => connection.close(), 500);
publishReading('meter123', 50.5, 'water');

Step 4: Consuming IoT Data in Microservices

Each subscribes to a queue.

Example for :

const amqp = require('amqplib');
async function consumeMessages() {    const connection = await amqp.connect('amqp://localhost');
    const channel = await connection.createChannel();
    const queue = 'water-meters';
    await channel.assertQueue(queue, { durable: true });
    console.log(`Waiting for messages in ${queue}`);
    channel.consume(queue, (msg) => {
        console.log(`Received: ${msg.content.toString()}`);
        channel.ack(msg);}

Key Takeaways from Using RabbitMQ in IoT

🚀 Improved System Scalability: Able to process per day.🔄 Reliable Message Delivery: No data loss, even when consumers go offline.📊 Efficient Data Processing: Microservices consume messages .🛠 Different queues handle .⚡ Ensures real-time IoT data processing.

Using in IoT systems provides a scalable, reliable, and efficient way to handle real-time data processing. Whether you’re monitoring smart meters, industrial sensors, or connected devices, a message queue ensures smooth data flow and fault tolerance.

💡 Are you working on an IoT project?💬 Let me know your biggest challenges in handling in the comments! 🚀

items.title: Spotlight on SIG etcd
items.content:
Frederico: Hello, thank you for the time! Let’s start with some introductions, could you tell us a bit about yourself, your role and how you got involved in Kubernetes.

Hello, I am Benjamin. I am a SIG etcd Tech Lead and one of the etcd maintainers. I work for VMware, which is part of the Broadcom group. I got involved in Kubernetes & etcd & CSI (Container Storage Interface) because of work and also a big passion for open source. I have been working on Kubernetes & etcd (and also CSI) since 2020.

Hey team, I’m James, a co-chair for SIG etcd and etcd maintainer. I work at Red Hat as a Specialist Architect helping people adopt cloud native technology. I got involved with the Kubernetes ecosystem in 2019. Around the end of 2022 I noticed how the etcd community and project needed help so started contributing as often as I could. There is a saying in our community that "you come for the technology, and stay for the people": for me this is absolutely real, it’s been a wonderful journey so far and I’m excited to support our community moving forward.

Hey everyone, I'm Marek, the SIG etcd lead. At Google, I lead the GKE etcd team, ensuring a stable and reliable experience for all GKE users. My Kubernetes journey began with SIG Instrumentation, where I created and led the Kubernetes Structured Logging effort. I'm still the main project lead for Kubernetes Metrics Server, providing crucial signals for autoscaling in Kubernetes. I started working on etcd 3 years ago, right around the 3.5 release. We faced some challenges, but I'm thrilled to see etcd now the most scalable and reliable it's ever been, with the highest contribution numbers in the project's history. I'm passionate about distributed systems, extreme programming, and testing.

Hi there, my name is Wenjia, I am the co-chair of SIG etcd and one of the etcd maintainers. I work at Google as an Engineering Manager, working on GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine) and GDC (Google Distributed Cloud). I have been working in the area of open source Kubernetes and etcd since the Kubernetes v1.10 and etcd v3.1 releases. I got involved in Kubernetes because of my job, but what keeps me in the space is the charm of the container orchestration technology, and more importantly, the awesome open source community.

Frederico: Excellent, thank you. I'd like to start with the origin of the SIG itself: SIG etcd is a very recent SIG, could you quickly go through the history and reasons behind its creation?

: Absolutely! SIG etcd was formed because etcd is a critical component of Kubernetes, serving as its data store. However, etcd was facing challenges like maintainer turnover and reliability issues. Creating a dedicated SIG allowed us to focus on addressing these problems, improving development and maintenance processes, and ensuring etcd evolves in sync with the cloud-native landscape.

Frederico: And has becoming a SIG worked out as expected? Better yet, are the motivations you just described being addressed, and to what extent?

: It's been a positive change overall. Becoming a SIG has brought more structure and transparency to etcd's development. We've adopted Kubernetes processes like KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals and PRRs (Production Readiness Reviews, which has improved our feature development and release cycle.

Frederico: On top of those, what would you single out as the major benefit that has resulted from becoming a SIG?

: The biggest benefits for me was adopting Kubernetes testing infrastructure, tools like Prow and TestGrid. For large projects like etcd there is just no comparison to the default GitHub tooling. Having known, easy to use, clear tools is a major boost to the etcd as it makes it much easier for Kubernetes contributors to also help etcd.

: Totally agree, while challenges remain, the SIG structure provides a solid foundation for addressing them and ensuring etcd's continued success as a critical component of the Kubernetes ecosystem.

The positive impact on the community is another crucial aspect of SIG etcd's success that I’d like to highlight. The Kubernetes SIG structure has created a welcoming environment for etcd contributors, leading to increased participation from the broader Kubernetes community. We have had greater collaboration with other SIGs like SIG API Machinery, SIG Scalability, SIG Testing, SIG Cluster Lifecycle, etc.

This collaboration helps ensure etcd's development aligns with the needs of the wider Kubernetes ecosystem. The formation of the etcd Operator Working Group under the joint effort between SIG etcd and SIG Cluster Lifecycle exemplifies this successful collaboration, demonstrating a shared commitment to improving etcd's operational aspects within Kubernetes.

Frederico: Since you mentioned collaboration, have you seen changes in terms of contributors and community involvement in recent months?

: Yes -- as showing in our unique PR author data we recently hit an all time high in March and are trending in a positive direction:

Frederico: That's quite telling, thank you. In terms of the near future, what are the current priorities for SIG etcd?

: Reliability is always top of mind -– we need to make sure etcd is rock-solid. We're also working on making etcd easier to use and manage for operators. And we have our sights set on making etcd a viable standalone solution for infrastructure management, not just for Kubernetes. Oh, and of course, scaling -– we need to ensure etcd can handle the growing demands of the cloud-native world.

: I agree that reliability should always be our top guiding principle. We need to ensure not only correctness but also compatibility. Additionally, we should continuously strive to improve the understandability and maintainability of etcd. Our focus should be on addressing the pain points that the community cares about the most.

Frederico: Are there any specific SIGs that you work closely with?

: SIG API Machinery, for sure – they own the structure of the data etcd stores, so we're constantly working together. And SIG Cluster Lifecycle – etcd is a key part of Kubernetes clusters, so we collaborate on the newly created etcd operator Working group.

: Other than SIG API Machinery and SIG Cluster Lifecycle that Marek mentioned above, SIG Scalability and SIG Testing is another group that we work closely with.

Frederico: In a more general sense, how would you list the key challenges for SIG etcd in the evolving cloud native landscape?

: Well, reliability is always a challenge when you're dealing with critical data. The cloud-native world is evolving so fast that scaling to meet those demands is a constant effort.

Frederico: We're almost at the end of our conversation, but for those interested in in etcd, how can they get involved?

: We'd love to have them! The best way to start is to join our SIG etcd meetings, follow discussions on the etcd-dev mailing list, and check out our GitHub issues. We're always looking for people to review proposals, test code, and contribute to documentation.

: I love this question 😀 . There are numerous ways for people interested in contributing to SIG etcd to get involved and make a difference. Here are some key areas where you can help:

  • : Tackle existing issues in the etcd codebase. Start with issues labeled "good first issue" or "help wanted" to find tasks that are suitable for newcomers.
  • : Contribute to the development of new features and enhancements. Check the etcd roadmap and discussions to see what's being planned and where your skills might fit in.
  • : Help ensure the quality of etcd by writing tests, reviewing code changes, and providing feedback.
  • : Improve etcd's documentation by adding new content, clarifying existing information, or fixing errors. Clear and comprehensive documentation is essential for users and contributors.
  • : Answer questions on forums, mailing lists, or Slack channels. Helping others understand and use etcd is a valuable contribution.
  • : Start by joining the etcd community on Slack, attending SIG meetings, and following the mailing lists. This will help you get familiar with the project, its processes, and the people involved.
  • : If you're new to open source or etcd, consider finding a mentor who can guide you and provide support. Stay tuned! Our first cohort of mentorship program was very successful. We will have a new round of mentorship program coming up.
  • : Don't be afraid to start with small contributions. Even fixing a typo in the documentation or submitting a simple bug fix can be a great way to get involved.

By contributing to etcd, you'll not only be helping to improve a critical piece of the cloud-native ecosystem but also gaining valuable experience and skills. So, jump in and start contributing!

Frederico: Excellent, thank you. Lastly, one piece of advice that you'd like to give to other newly formed SIGs?

: Absolutely! My advice would be to embrace the established processes of the larger community, prioritize collaboration with other SIGs, and focus on building a strong community.

: Here are some tips I myself found very helpful in my OSS journey:

  • : Open source development can take time. Don't get discouraged if your contributions aren't accepted immediately or if you encounter challenges.
  • : The etcd community values collaboration and respect. Be mindful of others' opinions and work together to achieve common goals.
  • : Contributing to open source should be enjoyable. Find areas that interest you and contribute in ways that you find fulfilling.

Frederico: A great way to end this spotlight, thank you all!

For more information and resources, please take a look at :
